Show that the quartic polynomial
x^4-7x^3+(2a+12)x^2-7ax+a^2
can be factored as a quadratic in x and a/x before clearing denominators. Specifically, verify that
x^2+\left(\frac{a}{x}\right)^2-7x-\frac{7a}{x}+2a+12
=
\left(x+\frac{a}{x}\right)^2-7\left(x+\frac{a}{x}\right)+12.
Then introduce y=x+\frac{a}{x} and restate the root condition in terms of y.
milestones_recovered.answer: The equation becomes y^2-7y+12=0 where y=x+\frac{a}{x}, so y=3 or y=4.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the identity is verified and the substitution y=x+a/x leads to y^2-7y+12=0 with solutions 3 and 4. ALLOW direct factoring as (y-3)(y-4)=0.
decompose_advice: First expand (x+a/x)^2 and compare term-by-term with the original equation.
Then solve the quadratic y^2-7y+12=0.
Restate that any root x must satisfy either x+a/x=3 or x+a/x=4.

milestones_recovered.description: For a fixed real number k, consider the equation x+\frac{a}{x}=k with x\neq 0. Show that this is equivalent to the quadratic equation x^2-kx+a=0. Also state the condition under which this quadratic has equal roots.
milestones_recovered.answer: It is equivalent to x^2-kx+a=0. This quadratic has equal roots exactly when its discriminant is 0, i.e. k^2-4a=0.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF x+a/x=k is converted to x^2-kx+a=0 and the repeated-root condition k^2-4a=0 is stated. ALLOW any equivalent discriminant argument.
hints: Multiply both sides by x to remove the denominator.
A quadratic ux^2+vx+w=0 has equal roots iff v^2-4uw=0.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the fact that any root x of the original equation must satisfy either x+\frac{a}{x}=3 or x+\frac{a}{x}=4. For each of the two cases, convert to a quadratic in x using x+\frac{a}{x}=k \iff x^2-kx+a=0, and impose the condition that this quadratic has equal roots. Find all corresponding values of a.
milestones_recovered.answer: For k=3, equal roots require 9-4a=0, so a=9/4. For k=4, equal roots require 16-4a=0, so a=4.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both cases are checked and the values a=9/4 and a=4 are obtained. ALLOW equivalent derivations.
hints: Case 1: x+a/x=3 gives x^2-3x+a=0.
Case 2: x+a/x=4 gives x^2-4x+a=0.
Set the discriminant equal to 0 in each case.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that each candidate value of a found from the repeated-root condition actually produces an equal root of the original equation, and that the repeated root is nonzero so the original equation is defined. Test a=9/4 and a=4 by finding the corresponding repeated x-value from x^2-kx+a=0.
milestones_recovered.answer: If a=9/4, then x^2-3x+9/4=0=(x-3/2)^2, so x=3/2\neq 0 is a repeated root. If a=4, then x^2-4x+4=0=(x-2)^2, so x=2\neq 0 is a repeated root.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both candidates are verified with repeated nonzero roots x=3/2 and x=2 respectively. ALLOW substitution back into x+a/x=k instead of the full original equation.
hints: Use the specific k paired with each a: k=3 for a=9/4 and k=4 for a=4.
Factor the resulting quadratic to read off the double root.

milestones_recovered.description: The original equation can be rewritten as \left(x+\frac{a}{x}\right)^2-7\left(x+\frac{a}{x}\right)+12=0. Letting y=x+\frac{a}{x}, we get y=3 or y=4. For a repeated root x, the corresponding quadratic x^2-yx+a=0 must itself have equal roots, so y^2-4a=0. Using y=3 and y=4 gives the candidate values a=9/4 and a=4, and both produce nonzero repeated roots x=3/2 and x=2 respectively. State the final answer.

Assume gcd(2x-3,x+2)=7 and (2x-3)(x+2) is a positive power of a prime. Since both factors are divisible by 7 and their product is a prime power, each factor must be a signed power of 7. Determine all integer x satisfying this condition.

milestones_recovered.description: Let n=2x^2+x-6. Rewrite the problem as: find all integers x for which there exist a prime number p and an integer k>=1 such that n=p^k. Also note that n factors as 2x^2+x-6=(x+2)(2x-3). State this factorization and the target equation (x+2)(2x-3)=p^k with p prime and k>=1.
milestones_recovered.answer: (x+2)(2x-3)=p^k for some prime p and integer k>=1.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly factors 2x^2+x-6 as (x+2)(2x-3) and restates the goal as equality to a positive prime power.

milestones_recovered.description: Let a and b be integers with ab=p^k, where p is prime and k>=1. Prove the following fact: if gcd(a,b)=1, then one of |a| or |b| equals 1. (Reason: every prime divisor of a or b must be p, but coprimeness prevents both from being divisible by p.)
milestones_recovered.answer: If ab=p^k and gcd(a,b)=1, then |a|=1 or |b|=1.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response establishes that both a and b can only have prime divisor p, and coprimeness forces at most one factor to contain p, so the other has absolute value 1.
hints: Any prime dividing a must also divide ab=p^k, so that prime is p; similarly for b.
If both |a|>1 and |b|>1, then p divides both a and b, contradicting gcd(a,b)=1.

milestones_recovered.description: For the two integer factors x+2 and 2x-3, compute gcd(x+2,2x-3). Show that any common divisor d of x+2 and 2x-3 must divide 2(x+2)-(2x-3)=7. Conclude that gcd(x+2,2x-3) is either 1 or 7.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume (x+2)(2x-3)=p^k with p prime and k>=1. Using the fact that gcd(x+2,2x-3) is either 1 or 7, split into cases. In the case gcd(x+2,2x-3)=1, use the fact: if ab=p^k and gcd(a,b)=1, then one of |a| or |b| equals 1. Translate this into explicit equations for x by setting x+2=±1 or 2x-3=±1, and list the integer x values obtained from these equations.
milestones_recovered.answer: From gcd=1, the only possibilities are x+2=±1 or 2x-3=±1, giving x=-3,-1,1,2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly applies the coprime prime-power lemma and solves the four linear equations to get exactly x=-3,-1,1,2.
decompose_advice: First isolate the gcd=1 case only.
Then separately solve x+2=1, x+2=-1, 2x-3=1, 2x-3=-1 over the integers.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the candidate integers x=-3,-1,1,2 in n=2x^2+x-6 and determine which values make n a positive prime power. Compute n explicitly for each x and reject any case where n is not positive.
milestones_recovered.answer: x=-3 gives 9=3^2; x=-1 gives -5 (reject); x=1 gives -3 (reject); x=2 gives 4=2^2. So from the gcd=1 case, the valid x are -3 and 2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the four substitutions are computed correctly and only x=-3 and x=2 are kept.

milestones_recovered.description: Now handle the case gcd(x+2,2x-3)=7 under the assumption (x+2)(2x-3)=p^k with p prime and k>=1. Since the gcd is 7, both factors are divisible by 7, so the prime p must be 7. Write x+2=7a and 2x-3=7b with integers a,b. Then ab=7^{k-2} and gcd(a,b)=1 because all common factors of x+2 and 2x-3 were exactly absorbed by dividing by 7. Using the fact: if ab=7^m and gcd(a,b)=1, then one of |a| or |b| equals 1, reduce to the equations x+2=±7 or 2x-3=±7 and solve for integer x.
milestones_recovered.answer: From gcd=7, necessarily p=7 and one gets x+2=±7 or 2x-3=±7, giving x=-9,5,-2,5; thus the distinct integer candidates are x=-9,-2,5.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response explains p=7, divides out a factor 7 from each term, applies the coprime prime-power lemma to the reduced factors, and solves to obtain candidates x=-9,-2,5.
decompose_advice: First justify carefully why p must equal 7 when both factors are divisible by 7 and their product is a prime power.
Then after setting x+2=7a and 2x-3=7b, prove gcd(a,b)=1 and apply the coprime lemma to get the reduced possibilities.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the candidate integers x=-9,-2,5 in n=2x^2+x-6 and determine which values make n a positive prime power. Compute n explicitly for each x.
milestones_recovered.answer: x=-9 gives 147=3·7^2 (reject); x=-2 gives 0 (reject); x=5 gives 49=7^2. So from the gcd=7 case, the only valid x is 5.

A valid output must be a positive integer equal to p^k for some prime p and k>=1.

milestones_recovered.description: Let triangle ABC have side lengths BC=a, AC=b, and AB=c. Let E be the midpoint of BC, so AE is a median, and let D be the midpoint of AC, so BD is a median. The medians AE and BD are perpendicular, and their squared lengths satisfy AE^2+BD^2=d^2. Write the standard formulas for the squared lengths of the medians to sides of lengths a and b: AE^2=(2b^2+2c^2-a^2)/4 and BD^2=(2a^2+2c^2-b^2)/4. Also write the condition expressing that AE and BD are perpendicular by using vectors from the centroid G, where AG:GE=BG:GD=2:1 on each median.
milestones_recovered.answer: AE^2=(2b^2+2c^2-a^2)/4, BD^2=(2a^2+2c^2-b^2)/4, and the perpendicular-median condition can be converted into a relation among a,b,c using centroid ratios.

milestones_recovered.description: Place the triangle in the coordinate plane with B=(-a/2,0), C=(a/2,0), and A=(x,y), where y>0. Then E=(0,0) because E is the midpoint of BC, and D=((x+a/2)/2,y/2) because D is the midpoint of AC. The median vectors are \overrightarrow{EA}=(x,y) and \overrightarrow{DB}=B-D=(-3a/4-x/2,-y/2), or equivalently one may use \overrightarrow{BD}=((x+a/2)/2+a/2,y/2)=((x/2)+(3a/4),y/2). Since AE is perpendicular to BD, their dot product is 0. Derive the resulting equation relating x, y, and a.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF a coordinate setup equivalent to the one stated is used and the orthogonality condition simplifies to 2x^2+3ax+2y^2=0; algebraically equivalent forms are allowed.

Set their dot product equal to 0 and multiply by 4.

Also compute the median lengths: AE^2=x^2+y^2 and BD^2=(x/2+3a/4)^2+(y/2)^2. Given the orthogonality relation 2x^2+3ax+2y^2=0, simplify the sum AE^2+BD^2 to an expression depending only on a and x, then use AE^2+BD^2=d^2 to solve for x.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver correctly gets a linear formula for x from d^2=AE^2+BD^2; equivalent simplified forms are allowed.
decompose_advice: First simplify BD^2 explicitly, then add AE^2.
Use 2x^2+3ax+2y^2=0 to replace x^2+y^2 by -3ax/2.

milestones_recovered.description: Continue with B=(-a/2,0), C=(a/2,0), A=(x,y). The orthogonality condition gives 2x^2+3ax+2y^2=0, so y^2=-(2x^2+3ax)/2. The sides satisfy AB^2=(x+a/2)^2+y^2 and AC^2=(x-a/2)^2+y^2. Substitute the expression for y^2 into these formulas and simplify each side length squared entirely in terms of a and x. Then substitute x=(16d^2-3a^2)/(3a) to obtain AB^2 and AC^2 in terms of a and d only.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both final formulas for AB^2 and AC^2 are correct, in either order, since the two unknown sides may be labeled symmetrically depending on coordinate choice.

Also check that AB^2+AC^2=8d^2/3+a^2/3, which is compatible with the median condition and shows the expressions are dimensionally consistent.
milestones_recovered.answer: Positivity requires \frac{a^2}{8}<d^2<\frac{3a^2}{8}; the formulas are dimensionally consistent.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the range \frac{a^2}{8}<d^2<\frac{3a^2}{8} (or equivalent inequalities for d) is given, with a brief consistency check.
hints: From AB^2>0 get d^2<3a^2/8.
From AC^2>0 get d^2>a^2/8.

milestones_recovered.description: Let BC=a. From the coordinate derivation with B=(-a/2,0), C=(a/2,0), A=(x,y), the perpendicularity of medians AE and BD gives 2x^2+3ax+2y^2=0. The condition AE^2+BD^2=d^2 then yields x=(16d^2-3a^2)/(3a). Substituting this into AB^2=(x+a/2)^2+y^2 and AC^2=(x-a/2)^2+y^2, together with y^2=-(2x^2+3ax)/2, gives AB^2=a^2-8d^2/3 and AC^2=(16d^2-2a^2)/3. Therefore state the other two sides of triangle ABC.
milestones_recovered.answer: AB=\sqrt{a^2-\frac{8d^2}{3}},\qquad AC=\sqrt{\frac{16d^2-2a^2}{3}}
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF these two side lengths are given, possibly swapped in order. No extra derivation is needed in this assembly step.
hints: The two unknown sides are determined up to swapping labels B and C relative to the coordinate choice.
Take positive square roots because side lengths are positive.

milestones_recovered.description: Model the cube as the set of 8 vertices with coordinates $(x,y,z)$ where each coordinate is either $0$ or $1$. Three random vertices $P,Q,R$ are chosen independently and uniformly from these 8 vertices. A face of the cube is exactly the set of 4 vertices obtained by fixing one coordinate to be $0$ or $1$; for example, $x=0$ is a face and similarly for $x=1,y=0,y=1,z=0,z=1$. Restate the event 'some face of the cube contains $P,Q,R$' as a condition on the coordinates of $P,Q,R$.
milestones_recovered.answer: The event occurs exactly when there exists one coordinate among $x,y,z$ such that the three chosen vertices all have the same value in that coordinate (all 0 or all 1).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer says a common face exists iff one of the three coordinates is constant across P,Q,R; ALLOW equivalent wording about all three lying in a set like x=0.
decompose_advice: Explicitly prove both directions: if a face contains the points then one coordinate is fixed, and if one coordinate is fixed then that face contains all three.
List the six faces in the coordinate model to justify the characterization.

milestones_recovered.description: For three independent fair coin flips, compute the probability that all three flips are equal. You may use that there are $2^3=8$ equally likely outcomes for the three flips.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the result is 1/4, or equivalently 2/8.
hints: The only successful outcomes are HHH and TTT.
There are 8 equally likely outcomes total.

milestones_recovered.description: Let $A_x$ be the event that the three random vertices $P,Q,R$ all have the same $x$-coordinate; define $A_y$ and $A_z$ analogously. Using the coordinate model where each vertex is chosen independently and uniformly from $(0,0,0),(0,0,1),\dots,(1,1,1)$, compute $P(A_x)$, $P(A_y)$, and $P(A_z)$.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF each of the three probabilities is given as 1/4.
hints: For a uniformly random cube vertex, each individual coordinate is an independent fair bit.
Apply the warmup fact to one coordinate across the three chosen vertices.

milestones_recovered.description: Let $A_x,A_y,A_z$ be the events that the three chosen cube vertices have a common value in the $x$-, $y$-, or $z$-coordinate respectively. Compute the probabilities of the pairwise intersections and the triple intersection: $P(A_x\cap A_y)$, $P(A_x\cap A_z)$, $P(A_y\cap A_z)$, and $P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z)$. A pairwise intersection means, for example, that all three vertices have the same $x$-coordinate and also the same $y$-coordinate.
milestones_recovered.answer: P(A_x\cap A_y)=P(A_x\cap A_z)=P(A_y\cap A_z)=1/16, and P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z)=1/64
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF all three pairwise intersections are 1/16 and the triple intersection is 1/64; ALLOW equivalent explanations such as 'then all three vertices lie on one line for pairwise intersections, and must be identical for the triple intersection'.
decompose_advice: For a pairwise intersection, first note there are 4 possible fixed pairs of coordinate values, such as x=0 and y=1; then count the allowed vertices and the probability that all three land among them.
For the triple intersection, explain that fixing x, y, and z determines a unique vertex, so all three chosen vertices must be equal.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the fact that the event 'some face contains $P,Q,R$' is exactly $A_x\cup A_y\cup A_z$, where $A_x,A_y,A_z$ are the events that the three vertices share a common $x$-, $y$-, or $z$-coordinate, write the probability of interest using inclusion-exclusion in terms of $P(A_x),P(A_y),P(A_z)$, the three pairwise intersection probabilities, and the triple intersection probability.
milestones_recovered.answer: P(A_x\cup A_y\cup A_z)=P(A_x)+P(A_y)+P(A_z)-P(A_x\cap A_y)-P(A_x\cap A_z)-P(A_y\cap A_z)+P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z)
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the standard 3-event inclusion-exclusion formula is written correctly for these events.
hints: For three events, add the singles, subtract the pairwise overlaps, then add back the triple overlap.
Do not compute numerically yet; just set up the exact formula.

milestones_recovered.description:
Use the inclusion-exclusion formula
$$P(A_x\cup A_y\cup A_z)=P(A_x)+P(A_y)+P(A_z)-P(A_x\cap A_y)-P(A_x\cap A_z)-P(A_y\cap A_z)+P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z),$$
and the values
$$P(A_x)=P(A_y)=P(A_z)=\frac14,$$
$$P(A_x\cap A_y)=P(A_x\cap A_z)=P(A_y\cap A_z)=\frac{1}{16},$$
$$P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z)=\frac{1}{64},$$
to compute the desired probability numerically.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final computed value is 37/64.
hints: Compute 3\cdot(1/4)-3\cdot(1/16)+1/64.
Convert to sixty-fourths: 48/64-12/64+1/64=37/64.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the value $37/64$ by counting the complement event directly. The complement is that for each of the three coordinates $x,y,z$, the three chosen vertices do not all have the same value in that coordinate. For three 0/1 bits, 'not all the same' means the multiset is two of one value and one of the other, so there are 6 ordered patterns out of 8. Verify that the complement probability is $(6/8)^3$ and hence the target probability is $1-(6/8)^3$.
milestones_recovered.answer: Complement probability = 27/64, so target probability = 1-27/64 = 37/64
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the complement is computed as 27/64 and the final probability as 37/64; ALLOW any equivalent direct-count sanity check.
hints: The three coordinates across the three chosen vertices are independent collections of three fair bits.
For one coordinate, the bad event excludes only 000 and 111.

milestones_recovered.description: In the coordinate model of the cube, a common face contains $P,Q,R$ exactly when one of the coordinates $x,y,z$ is constant across the three chosen vertices. Thus the desired event is $A_x\cup A_y\cup A_z$. Using the computed values $P(A_x)=P(A_y)=P(A_z)=1/4$, $P(A_x\cap A_y)=P(A_x\cap A_z)=P(A_y\cap A_z)=1/16$, and $P(A_x\cap A_y\cap A_z)=1/64$, assemble them with inclusion-exclusion to give the final probability requested in the original problem.
milestones_recovered.answer: \boxed{\frac{37}{64}}
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer is exactly 37/64, with or without boxing.

milestones_recovered.description: Let gcd(a,b)=15 and ab=7875, where a and b are natural numbers with 0<a<b. Write a=15x and b=15y, where x and y are natural numbers. Because gcd(a,b)=15, the numbers x and y satisfy gcd(x,y)=1. Substitute into ab=7875 to obtain an equation for x and y, and restate the original task as finding all coprime natural-number pairs (x,y) with x<y that satisfy that equation.
milestones_recovered.answer: a=15x, b=15y with gcd(x,y)=1, x<y, and xy=7875/225=35. So the problem becomes: find all coprime natural-number pairs (x,y) with x<y and xy=35.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the reformulation reaches xy=35 together with gcd(x,y)=1 and x<y, and identifies (a,b)=(15x,15y).
decompose_advice: First justify why 15 divides both a and b from gcd(a,b)=15.
Then divide the product equation by 15^2=225 carefully to get xy=35.

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose x and y are positive integers with gcd(x,y)=1 and xy=35. Since 35=5·7 is squarefree, prove that each of x and y must be a product of a disjoint subset of the prime factors {5,7}. Deduce all coprime pairs (x,y) of positive integers with xy=35 and x<y.

decompose_advice: Factor 35 into primes and note that no prime factor can appear in both x and y because gcd(x,y)=1.
Then enumerate the divisors x of 35 and keep only those giving x<35/x and gcd(x,35/x)=1.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the substitutions a=15x and b=15y, compute the corresponding pairs (a,b) for each coprime pair (x,y) with x<y and xy=35. The pairs to use are (x,y)=(1,35) and (x,y)=(5,7).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both resulting pairs are computed correctly.
hints: Multiply each coordinate by 15.
Keep the order so that a<b.

milestones_recovered.answer: Both pairs satisfy all conditions.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response verifies gcd(15,525)=15 and 15·525=7875, and also gcd(75,105)=15 and 75·105=7875, with a<b in both cases.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider an n×n grid whose cells are labeled either +1 or -1. Two cells are called adjacent if they share a side. Let deg_opp(c) denote, for a cell c, the number of adjacent cells having the opposite sign from c. The condition in the problem is exactly that deg_opp(c)=1 for every cell c in the grid. Restate the task as a graph-coloring problem on the n×n grid graph: vertices are cells, edges join side-adjacent cells, and each vertex must have exactly one neighbor of the opposite color. What is the formal restatement of the original problem in these graph terms?
milestones_recovered.answer: Find all positive integers n for which the n×n grid graph admits a 2-coloring of its vertices by signs ±1 such that every vertex has exactly one neighbor of the opposite sign.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer gives an equivalent graph formulation with vertices = cells, edges = side adjacencies, two colors/signs, and the condition 'each vertex has exactly one opposite-colored neighbor'.
decompose_advice: Define the grid graph explicitly and translate the cell condition into a vertex-neighbor condition.
State the final goal in terms of existence of such a 2-coloring for given n.

milestones_recovered.description: Let G be any finite graph. Suppose each vertex of G is colored by +1 or -1, and each vertex has exactly one neighbor of the opposite color. Let E_cross be the set of edges whose endpoints have opposite colors. Prove that in the spanning subgraph (V(G),E_cross), every vertex has degree 1, and therefore E_cross is a perfect matching of G. Also prove the converse: if a graph has a perfect matching M, then coloring each matched edge with opposite signs on its two endpoints and requiring equal signs across every non-matching edge would produce a valid labeling exactly when those equalities are globally consistent.
milestones_recovered.answer: The opposite-sign edges form a 1-regular spanning subgraph, hence a perfect matching. Conversely, a valid labeling must come from a perfect matching of opposite-sign edges together with consistency that every non-matching edge joins equal signs.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it states and justifies that 'exactly one opposite neighbor per vertex' means exactly one incident opposite-sign edge per vertex, so the cross edges form a perfect matching; and mentions the converse consistency requirement for non-matching edges.
decompose_advice: First show each vertex is incident to exactly one opposite-sign edge.
Then conclude the cross-edge subgraph is 1-regular on all vertices, i.e. a perfect matching.
Finally state the converse carefully, including the need that non-matching edges connect equal signs.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the fact that any valid labeling forces the opposite-sign edges to form a perfect matching of the n×n grid graph. Deduce a necessary parity condition on n. Recall that a graph with a perfect matching must have an even number of vertices. What necessary condition on n follows for an n×n grid?
milestones_recovered.answer: n must be even, because the grid has n^2 vertices and a perfect matching requires n^2 to be even.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it concludes n is even. Any correct parity argument via perfect matching is fine.
hints: A perfect matching partitions the vertex set into disjoint pairs.
So the number of vertices n^2 must be even.

milestones_recovered.description: For the 2×2 grid, explicitly assign +1 or -1 to each cell so that every cell has exactly one adjacent cell of the opposite sign. Give one valid pattern.
milestones_recovered.answer: For example, label the top row + + and the bottom row - -. Then each cell has exactly one opposite-sign neighbor, namely the vertical neighbor in its column.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF any valid 2×2 pattern is given and correctly checked.
hints: Try making each row constant and the two rows opposite.
Then horizontal neighbors have the same sign and vertical neighbors have opposite signs.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume n is even. Partition the n×n grid into n/2 horizontal strips, each strip consisting of two consecutive rows: rows 1 and 2, rows 3 and 4, and so on. In each 2-row strip, label every cell in the upper row by +1 and every cell in the lower row by -1. Verify directly, using only this construction, how many opposite-sign neighbors and how many same-sign neighbors each cell has depending on whether it is a corner, a non-corner boundary cell, or an interior cell.
milestones_recovered.answer: In this construction, every cell has exactly one opposite-sign neighbor: the cell directly above or below it within its 2-row strip. All other adjacent cells, if any, have the same sign.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it checks the proposed strip construction and concludes each cell has exactly one opposite-sign adjacent cell.
decompose_advice: State the explicit labeling rule for every row.

Check first a cell not on the top or bottom boundary of a strip: its vertical neighbor inside the strip has opposite sign, while horizontal neighbors and any vertical neighbor in the adjacent strip have the same sign.
Then check edge and corner cells separately to confirm the count remains exactly one.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the explicit construction: for even n, let rows with odd index be entirely +1 and rows with even index be entirely -1. Determine whether this labeling satisfies the condition that each cell has exactly one adjacent cell of the opposite sign, and state the resulting existence claim for all even n.
milestones_recovered.answer: Yes. For every even n, this row-constant alternating-by-pairs construction gives a valid labeling, so a solution exists for every even n.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it concludes existence for all even n via the stated construction. The checker may allow the equivalent construction with columns instead of rows.
hints: Because n is even, rows can be grouped into pairs (1,2),(3,4),... .
Within each pair, the two rows have opposite signs; adjacent rows from different pairs have the same sign.

milestones_recovered.description: Combine these two established facts: (1) in any valid labeling, the edges joining opposite signs form a perfect matching of the n×n grid graph, so n^2 must be even and therefore n must be even; (2) when n is even, labeling rows in consecutive pairs by +1 on the first row of each pair and -1 on the second row of each pair gives a valid configuration in which every cell has exactly one opposite-sign neighbor. Using only these facts, determine all positive integers n for which the required labeling exists.
milestones_recovered.answer: Exactly the even positive integers n.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer is 'all even n' or an equivalent phrase such as 'n is even'.
hints: One fact gives necessity: odd n are impossible.
The other fact gives sufficiency: every even n works.

milestones_recovered.description: In triangle ABC, the interior angles satisfy A+B+C=\pi and 0<A,B,C<\pi. milestones_recovered.description: Use the triangle angle relation A+B=\pi-C to replace \sin\frac{A+B}{2} by a function of C. Then transform the equation \frac{13}{5}\sin^2\frac{A+B}{2}+\cos^2\frac{A-B}{2}=\frac{9}{5} into an explicit formula for \cos^2\frac{A-B}{2} in terms of C alone.

milestones_recovered.answer: The existence condition is s\le \frac{13s-4}{5}\le 1, which gives \frac12\le s\le \frac{9}{13}. Hence \sin^2\frac C2\le \frac{9}{13}, so the maximum occurs at \sin^2\frac C2=\frac{9}{13}, i.e. C_{\max}=2\arcsin\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}=2\arctan\frac{3}{2}.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the candidate maximum angle C=2\arctan\frac32 is actually achievable and is consistent with triangle angle conditions. milestones_recovered.description: Let L, M, S be nonnegative integers representing the numbers of large, medium, and small boxes bought, where each large box contains 13 marbles, each medium box contains 11 marbles, and each small box contains 7 marbles. A customer can buy exactly n marbles without opening boxes if and only if the equation 13L + 11M + 7S = n has a solution in nonnegative integers L, M, S. The problem asks for the smallest integer N such that every integer n > N can be written in the form 13L + 11M + 7S with L, M, S >= 0. Restate the given examples 20 and 23 in this language and state the target formally.
milestones_recovered.answer: 20 is representable because 20 = 13 + 7, while 23 is not representable by 13L + 11M + 7S with L, M, S >= 0. The target is to find the largest nonrepresentable integer N; then the required smallest threshold is N.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response defines representability as n = 13L+11M+7S with L,M,S nonnegative integers, notes 20 representable and 23 not representable, and identifies the goal as finding the largest nonrepresentable integer.

milestones_recovered.description: Prove the following standalone claim: if seven consecutive integers N, N+1, ..., N+6 are each expressible as 13L + 11M + 7S with L, M, S >= 0, then every integer n >= N is also expressible in that form. You may use that adding one small box increases the total by 7 marbles.

milestones_recovered.description: Let triangle ABC be arbitrary with BC=a>0. Let O1 be the circumcenter of triangle ABC and let M lie on the ray from C through A such that angle MBC equals angle BAC. Introduce standard angle notation: alpha = angle BAC, beta = angle ABC, gamma = angle ACB, so alpha+beta+gamma=pi. Because M lies on ray [CA), points C,A,M are collinear and A is between C and M or equal to C excluded, hence angle BCM = angle BCA = gamma. Using the condition angle MBC = alpha, first determine the angles of triangle BCM and express BM and the circumradius R2 of triangle ABM in terms of a, alpha, beta, gamma. Also express the circumradius R1 of triangle ABC in terms of a and alpha.
milestones_recovered.answer: In triangle BCM, the angles are angle MBC=alpha, angle BCM=gamma, angle BMC=beta. Hence by the sine law, BM/sin gamma = BC/sin beta = a/sin beta, so BM = a sin gamma / sin beta. Since angle BAM = pi-alpha, the circumradius of triangle ABM is R2 = BM/(2 sin angle BAM) = BM/(2 sin alpha) = a sin gamma /(2 sin alpha sin beta). For triangle ABC, R1 = a/(2 sin alpha).

milestones_recovered.description: Use the following fact: for any two circles passing through the same distinct points A and B, their centers lie on the perpendicular bisector of AB. Therefore, if O1 and O2 are the circumcenters of triangles ABC and ABM, then O1 and O2 both lie on the perpendicular bisector of AB, and O1O2 equals the absolute difference of their perpendicular distances from line AB. For a circle through A and B with radius R, if the chord length AB is fixed, then the perpendicular distance from the center to line AB equals sqrt(R^2-(AB/2)^2). Apply this to triangles ABC and ABM. Using the side-angle relation in triangle ABC, AB = c = a sin gamma / sin alpha. Show that O1O2 can be written purely in terms of a, alpha, beta, gamma as O1O2 = (a/(2 sin alpha)) |cos alpha - (sin gamma/sin beta) cos(pi-alpha)|, and simplify this expression.

Using AB = a sin gamma/sin alpha, R1 = a/(2 sin alpha), and R2 = a sin gamma/(2 sin alpha sin beta), we get d1 = a cos alpha /(2 sin alpha) and d2 = a sin gamma cos alpha /(2 sin alpha sin beta). Hence O1O2 = (a cos alpha /(2 sin alpha)) |1 - sin gamma/sin beta|.

Equivalent use of cotangent is allowed. Because alpha is acute/obtuse in some cases, an absolute value is necessary somewhere.

Another substep is reducing the square roots: sqrt(1-sin^2 gamma)=|cos gamma| is not the one needed; use c=a sin gamma/sin alpha and simplify directly to d1=R1|cos alpha|, d2=R2|cos(pi-alpha)|.

Deduce that sin gamma / sin beta = sin alpha cot beta + cos alpha. milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the sine-addition identity is used correctly and the final equivalent form is obtained. Any algebraically equivalent expression is acceptable.

milestones_recovered.description: Let P(n) denote the statement: for every positive divisor d of the positive integer n, the integer d+1 divides n+1. milestones_recovered.answer: The problem is to find all positive integers n with P(n). From d=1 we get 2 | (n+1), so n is odd. From d=n we get n+1 | n+1, which is automatically true.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume n is a positive integer satisfying P(n): for every positive divisor d of n, d+1 divides n+1. Suppose p is a prime divisor of n. Since p divides n, it is also a positive divisor of n, so p+1 divides n+1. Prove that gcd(p,p+1)=1 and then deduce from p | n and p+1 | n+1 that p cannot divide p+1 and hence p cannot divide n+1. Use this to show that p+1 is not divisible by p.
milestones_recovered.answer: For any prime divisor p of n, gcd(p,p+1)=1, so p does not divide p+1. Also p | n and 2 | (n+1), but p+1 | n+1 gives a divisor of n+1 coprime to p; in particular p ∤ (p+1).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it is noted that consecutive integers are coprime and therefore p cannot divide p+1. This warmup is mainly a basic coprimality check.
hints: Consecutive integers always have gcd 1.
If p is prime, then the only multiples of p among nearby integers are easy to identify.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume n is a positive integer satisfying P(n). Let p be any prime divisor of n, and let p^a be the exact power of p dividing n, where a>=1. Because p^a is a positive divisor of n, P(n) gives p^a+1 | n+1. Since also p | n, we have n ≡ 0 (mod p), hence n+1 ≡ 1 (mod p). Reduce the divisibility p^a+1 | n+1 modulo p and prove that this forces p^a+1 ≡ 1 (mod p), which is equivalent to p | p^a, impossible unless p=1. Instead, use the weaker divisor d=p to show p+1 | n+1 and combine n ≡ 0 (mod p) with n+1 ≡ 0 (mod p+1) to derive the congruence p ≡ -1 (mod p+1), then conclude p+1 divides 1 more carefully by expressing n=pk and comparing modulo p+1. The target of this milestone is the correct restriction: every prime divisor p of n must satisfy p=2 or p=1 is impossible, so for odd n there can be no odd prime divisor.
milestones_recovered.answer: If p is a prime divisor of n, then using d=n/p (when p|n) gives n/p + 1 | n+1, and comparing modulo that divisor leads to p dividing 1; equivalently, no odd prime can divide n. Since n is odd from d=1, this forces n=1.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver derives the crucial contradiction for any prime divisor p of n, showing n cannot have a prime divisor. Equivalent correct routes are allowed, e.g. taking a smallest prime divisor p and using d=n/p so that n+1-(p)(n/p+1)=1-p, hence n/p+1 divides p-1, then n/p+1>p-1 unless n=p, and the case n=p also fails for odd prime p because p+1 ∤ p+1? Actually for n=p it holds only for d=1,p, so p+1 divides p+1; then use d=1 to get p odd and test p=3 fails? Verifier should accept any valid contradiction that no odd prime divisor exists.
decompose_advice: A clean route is: choose the smallest prime divisor p of n and set d=n/p. Since d is a divisor of n, d+1 divides n+1. Compute n+1-p(d+1)=1-p, so d+1 divides p-1.
Then compare sizes: because p is the smallest prime divisor of n, either n=p or d=n/p >= p, hence d+1>p-1. This forces n=p. Finish by checking which prime n can satisfy P(n).

milestones_recovered.description: Carry out the size argument hinted in the previous milestone in a fully standalone way. Assume n>1 satisfies P(n). Let p be the smallest prime divisor of n, and define d=n/p, which is a positive divisor of n. Then d+1 divides n+1. Since n=pd, compute (n+1)-p(d+1)=1-p, so d+1 divides p-1. Now use the fact that p is the smallest prime divisor of n to show d=n/p is either 1 or at least p. Deduce that if d>=p then d+1>=p+1>p-1, impossible because a positive divisor of p-1 cannot exceed p-1. Therefore d=1, so n=p is prime. Finally test prime n in P(n): the only positive divisors of a prime p are 1 and p, so P(p) holds exactly when 2 divides p+1, i.e. when p is odd, and also for p=2 check directly. Combine with the earlier parity condition from d=1 to list the prime possibilities.
milestones_recovered.answer: Any n>1 satisfying P(n) must be prime. For a prime n=p, P(p) holds for all odd primes p (divisors are only 1 and p), while p=2 fails because d=1 would require 2 | 3. So all odd primes satisfy P(n).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver gets 'n must be prime' via d=n/p and the divisor-size contradiction, and then correctly checks that every odd prime works while 2 does not.

Do not skip the check that an odd prime indeed satisfies the original divisor condition for both of its divisors.

The condition 'the difference between any two adjacent digits is equal to 2' means that for every k=1,2,\dots,99, we have |a_{k+1}-a_k|=2. Restate the counting problem as: how many length-100 sequences (a_1,\dots,a_{100}) satisfy a_k\in{1,3,5,7,9} for all k and |a_{k+1}-a_k|=2 for all k?
milestones_recovered.answer: Count length-100 sequences on the digit set {1,3,5,7,9} with adjacent difference exactly 2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the problem is reformulated as counting 100-term digit sequences over {1,3,5,7,9} with |a_{k+1}-a_k|=2 for each adjacent pair.
decompose_advice: Define the allowed digit set and the adjacency rule explicitly.
Make clear that counting numbers is equivalent to counting such digit sequences, since all allowed first digits are nonzero.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider the graph whose vertices are the odd digits {1,3,5,7,9}, with an edge between two digits exactly when their difference is 2. List all allowed moves between consecutive digits. Then explain why a valid 100-digit number is exactly the same thing as a walk of length 99 in this graph, where the visited vertices are the digits a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}.
milestones_recovered.answer: The graph is the path 1-3-5-7-9, and valid 100-digit numbers correspond exactly to walks of length 99 on this path graph.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the allowed adjacencies are identified as 1↔3, 3↔5, 5↔7, 7↔9 and the walk interpretation on the 5-vertex path is stated.
decompose_advice: First list the possible next digits from each odd digit.
Then translate the digit condition into a walk-counting problem on a path graph.

milestones_recovered.description: For the path graph 1-3-5-7-9, color the vertices alternately in two colors according to parity of the index in the ordered list: put 1,5,9 in one class and 3,7 in the other class. Prove the following fact: every step along an edge moves from one color class to the other, so after an odd number of steps a walk starting in {1,5,9} ends in {3,7}, and a walk starting in {3,7} ends in {1,5,9}. Apply this specifically to walks of length 99.
milestones_recovered.answer: After 99 steps, a walk must end in the opposite color class: starts in {1,5,9} end in {3,7}, and starts in {3,7} end in {1,5,9}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the bipartition {1,5,9} and {3,7} is given and the color-switching-at-each-step argument for 99 odd steps is correct.
hints: Each allowed move changes the digit by 2, so it moves one place left or right along the path 1-3-5-7-9.
On a bipartite graph, every edge switches sides; 99 is odd, so the endpoint is on the opposite side from the start.

milestones_recovered.description: In the path graph 1-3-5-7-9, prove that for any starting vertex and any target vertex in the opposite color class, there is exactly one walk of length 99 from the start to the target. You may use the following explicit structure of the graph: 1 and 9 have degree 1, while 3,5,7 have degree 2. Show that once the first step is chosen, every later step is forced if the walk is to have total length 99 and finish at a prescribed opposite-color target; equivalently, along a path graph a walk of odd length between opposite-color vertices is uniquely determined by its endpoints when the length is at least the distance and has the same parity as that distance. Then specialize to length 99 on this 5-vertex path.
milestones_recovered.answer: For each ordered pair (start, end) with end in the opposite color class from start, there is exactly one walk of length 99 between them on 1-3-5-7-9.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the result 'exactly one 99-step walk for each start/end pair in opposite classes' is established with a valid path-graph forcing argument. Minor variation in proof is fine.
decompose_advice: Split by starting digit and inspect the deterministic back-and-forth behavior on the path.
Or prove a more general statement for a finite path graph: for sufficiently long walks, the endpoint in the opposite bipartition uniquely determines the walk.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the fact that valid numbers correspond to walks of length 99 on the path graph 1-3-5-7-9, and using the fact that after 99 steps the endpoint must lie in the opposite color class, count the total number of such walks by counting ordered pairs (start digit, end digit) with opposite colors. The color classes are {1,5,9} and {3,7}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the count is computed as 3·2 + 2·3 = 12, or equivalently there are 6 opposite-color ordered pairs in each direction, total 12.
hints: There are 3 possible starts in {1,5,9}, each with 2 possible ends in {3,7}.
There are 2 possible starts in {3,7}, each with 3 possible ends in {1,5,9}. Add the two counts.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the result against smaller lengths. For 2-digit numbers under the same rule, list all valid numbers and count them. Then compare with the ordered-pair method on walks of length 1 on the path graph 1-3-5-7-9. The two counts should agree.
milestones_recovered.answer: There are 8 such 2-digit numbers: 13, 31, 35, 53, 57, 75, 79, 97.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF all 8 numbers are listed or an equivalent count of 8 is given, and this is recognized as matching the graph method for 1-step walks.
hints: A 2-digit number corresponds to one edge traversal.
The undirected edges are 1-3, 3-5, 5-7, 7-9; each gives two directed 2-digit numbers.

milestones_recovered.description: A valid 100-digit number is the same as a walk of length 99 on the path graph 1-3-5-7-9. After 99 steps, the walk ends in the opposite bipartition class, where the classes are {1,5,9} and {3,7}. For each ordered pair (start digit, end digit) in opposite classes, there is exactly one such 99-step walk. Therefore the total count equals the number of ordered opposite-class pairs, namely 3·2 + 2·3 = 12. State the final answer to the original problem.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer is exactly 12, supported by assembling the walk model, opposite-class endpoint fact, and pair count; no new reasoning needed beyond those stated facts.
hints: Count ordered pairs of start and end digits in opposite color classes.
There are 6 pairs from {1,5,9} to {3,7} and 6 in the reverse direction.

milestones_recovered.description: Let q_1(x)=a x^{2}+2bx+1 and q_2(x)=b x^{2}+2ax+1, where a,b are real numbers. Translate the condition in the problem into algebraic constraints: each quadratic equation q_1(x)=0 and q_2(x)=0 must have two distinct real roots, and the two equations must have exactly one common real root. Write these requirements as conditions on discriminants and on a common root r satisfying both equations.
milestones_recovered.answer: There exists a real number r such that a r^2+2br+1=0 and b r^2+2ar+1=0; each equation must be genuinely quadratic, so a≠0 and b≠0; each must have two distinct real roots, so 4(b^2-a)>0 and 4(a^2-b)>0, equivalently b^2>a and a^2>b; and the two quadratics have exactly one common root.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response identifies a common root r satisfying both equations and the distinct-real-root conditions b^2>a, a^2>b together with a≠0, b≠0. Minor equivalent formulations are fine.
decompose_advice: Separate the translation into three pieces: nonzero leading coefficients, discriminant conditions, and existence of exactly one shared root.
Explicitly name the common root r and write the two equations it satisfies.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume there is a real number r common to both equations a r^2+2br+1=0 and b r^2+2ar+1=0. Subtract the second equation from the first and factor the result completely. Deduce all possibilities for a,b,r from this subtraction alone.
milestones_recovered.answer: Subtracting gives (a-b)r^2+2(b-a)r=(a-b)r(r-2)=0. Hence either a=b, or r=0, or r=2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the factorization (a-b)r(r-2)=0 is obtained and the three cases a=b or r=0 or r=2 are listed. Equivalent algebra is fine.
hints: Compute [a r^2+2br+1]-[b r^2+2ar+1].
Factor (a-b)r^2-2(a-b)r as (a-b)r(r-2).

milestones_recovered.description: Show that the cases a=b and r=0 cannot satisfy the original condition that each equation has two distinct real roots and exactly one common root. Use only the following facts: if a=b, then the two equations are identical; if r=0 is a common root, then substituting x=0 into a x^2+2bx+1 and b x^2+2ax+1 gives the constant term 1.
milestones_recovered.answer: Neither a=b nor r=0 is possible. If a=b, the two equations are the same and therefore have the same two roots, not exactly one common root. If r=0 were common, then 1=0 from substitution, impossible. Therefore any common root must be r=2 and a≠b.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both exclusions are justified and the conclusion 'the common root must be 2' is stated. The contradiction for r=0 must explicitly use the constant term 1.
hints: Identical quadratic equations cannot have exactly one common root if each has two distinct roots.
At x=0, both left-hand sides equal 1.

milestones_recovered.description: Now use the necessary fact that the unique common root is r=2. Substitute x=2 into both equations a x^2+2bx+1=0 and b x^2+2ax+1=0, and solve the resulting linear system for a and b.
milestones_recovered.answer: Substituting x=2 gives 4a+4b+1=0 and 4b+4a+1=0, so the only condition is a+b=-1/4.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver substitutes x=2 correctly and concludes a+b=-1/4. It is fine to note both equations become the same equation.
hints: At x=2, both equations reduce to 4a+4b+1=0.
So every candidate pair must lie on the line a+b=-1/4.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume a+b=-1/4. Then q_1(2)=q_2(2)=0, so x=2 is a common root. For a quadratic c x^2+d x+e=0 with one root r and c≠0, the other root is e/(c r) because the product of the roots is e/c. Apply this to q_1(x)=a x^2+2bx+1 and q_2(x)=b x^2+2ax+1 to express their second roots in terms of a and b, and impose the condition that x=2 is the only common root.
milestones_recovered.answer: For q_1, the product of roots is 1/a, so if one root is 2 then the other is 1/(2a). For q_2, the other root is 1/(2b). Thus the equations have exactly one common root iff 1/(2a)≠1/(2b), i.e. a≠b.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the second roots are identified as 1/(2a) and 1/(2b) and the exactly-one-common-root condition is converted to a≠b. The response should implicitly or explicitly note a,b≠0.
hints: Use 'product of roots = constant term / leading coefficient'.
The shared root 2 is unique exactly when the other roots are different.

milestones_recovered.description: Under the line condition a+b=-1/4, determine exactly when each quadratic has two distinct real roots. You may use either discriminants or the second-root formulas from the previous step. Give the final condition on a and b in a simple form.
milestones_recovered.answer: Since one root is 2, q_1 has two distinct real roots iff its other root 1/(2a) is real and not equal to 2, which means a≠0 and a≠1/4. Likewise q_2 has two distinct real roots iff b≠0 and b≠1/4. Together with a+b=-1/4, this is equivalent to a+b=-1/4 and a,b are both nonzero and unequal; equivalently a+b=-1/4 and a≠b (because on this line, a=0 forces b=-1/4 and then q_2 is linear, and a=b forces a=b=-1/8, giving identical equations). A convenient final form is a+b=-1/4 and a≠b.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver concludes that along a+b=-1/4 the needed condition reduces to a≠b, with a correct justification that this ensures both are nonzero and both quadratics have two distinct real roots. Equivalent formulations such as 'a+b=-1/4, a≠0, b≠0, a≠b' are acceptable.
decompose_advice: First show distinctness by comparing the second root with 2: 1/(2a)≠2 and 1/(2b)≠2.
Then simplify the side conditions using b=-1/4-a to see that a≠b already excludes the degenerate identical-root/identical-equation cases on this line.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the candidate family a+b=-1/4 with a≠b by testing a concrete example, for instance a=1 and b=-5/4. Verify directly that both equations have two distinct real roots and exactly one common root.
milestones_recovered.answer: For (a,b)=(1,-5/4), the equations are x^2-(5/2)x+1=0 and (-5/4)x^2+2x+1=0. The first has roots 2 and 1/2; the second has roots 2 and -2/5. So both have two distinct real roots and the only common root is 2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF a valid example on the line a+b=-1/4 with a≠b is checked correctly and yields exactly one shared root. Any correct example is fine.
hints: Choose any unequal real numbers summing to -1/4.
Factor using the known common root x=2.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the established facts: a common root r must satisfy (a-b)r(r-2)=0; the cases a=b and r=0 are impossible under the requirement of exactly one common root, so the common root must be 2. Substituting x=2 into both equations gives a+b=-1/4. Along this line, each quadratic has roots 2 and 1/(2a) for the first equation, and 2 and 1/(2b) for the second, so they have exactly one common root precisely when a≠b; this also guarantees the equations are not identical and each has two distinct real roots. State the complete set of all pairs (a,b).
milestones_recovered.answer: All such pairs are exactly the real pairs satisfying a+b=-\tfrac14 and a\ne b.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer is exactly the set {(a,b)\in\mathbb R^2: a+b=-1/4, a\ne b}, or an equivalent description.
hints: The common root is forced to be 2.
Then the only remaining conditions are the line equation a+b=-1/4 and the uniqueness condition a≠b.

milestones_recovered.description: Let the chessboard be the set of unit squares with coordinates (i,j) where 1≤i,j≤n, and color square (i,j) black when i+j is even and white when i+j is odd. Since n is odd, the four corners are black. A tromino is an L-shape consisting of a 2×2 block with exactly one of its four unit squares missing, so every tromino covers exactly three squares. Determine two basic counting facts needed later: (1) the number of black squares on the n×n board, and (2) in any such L-tromino placed on the checkerboard, how many black squares and how many white squares it covers.
milestones_recovered.answer: The board has (n^2+1)/2 black squares. Every L-tromino covers exactly 2 squares of one color and 1 square of the other color.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer states black count is (n^2+1)/2 and explains that any 2×2 block has two black and two white squares, so removing one square leaves either 2 black + 1 white or 2 white + 1 black.
decompose_advice: Split the counting of black squares and the color-content of one tromino into separate milestones if needed.
A coordinate-parity argument for the board and a 2×2-block argument for the tromino are natural substeps.

milestones_recovered.description: A black square can be covered only by a tromino that contains at least one black square. Since every L-tromino on a checkerboard covers either 2 black and 1 white squares or 1 black and 2 white squares, classify trominoes into type A = covers 2 black squares and 1 white square, and type B = covers 1 black square and 2 white squares. If x and y are the numbers of type A and type B trominoes in a covering of all black squares, write the exact equation forced by covering every black square of the n×n board, where n is odd.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF x,y are defined as the two color-types of trominoes and the black-square covering equation 2x+y=(n^2+1)/2 is derived.
hints: Count black squares covered by all trominoes in two ways.
Type A contributes 2 black squares; type B contributes 1 black square.

milestones_recovered.description: For an odd integer n, determine exactly when the Diophantine equation 2x+y=(n^2+1)/2 has a solution in nonnegative integers x,y with total number of trominoes x+y equal to (n^2+1)/6. Express the condition as a congruence on n modulo 6.
milestones_recovered.answer: This is possible exactly when n^2+1 is divisible by 6, which for odd n is equivalent to n not being divisible by 3, i.e. n ≡ 1 or 5 (mod 6).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it concludes divisibility by 6 is required and for odd n this means n mod 6 is 1 or 5. A correct modular check mod 3 is sufficient.
decompose_advice: First show that any covering needs at least (n^2+1)/6 trominoes because each tromino covers at most 2 black squares.
Then analyze when this lower bound is an integer using n^2+1 modulo 6 for odd n.

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose all black squares of an odd n×n checkerboard are covered by non-overlapping L-trominoes. Let B=(n^2+1)/2 be the number of black squares. Since each tromino covers at most 2 black squares, prove a general lower bound for the number T of trominoes, and determine the value of T in the best possible case. Also state what must then be true about the color-type of every tromino in an optimal covering.
milestones_recovered.answer: T≥ceil(B/2)=ceil((n^2+1)/4). In the best possible case with equality to B/2, every tromino must be type A, covering exactly 2 black and 1 white. Since B/2=(n^2+1)/4 is not always integral, the sharp global lower bound from black-counting is T≥ceil((n^2+1)/4).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it correctly derives T≥ceil((n^2+1)/4) from 'at most 2 black per tromino'. Also accept mention that equality forces every tromino to cover 2 black squares.
decompose_advice: Separate the lower-bound argument from the equality characterization.
If needed, explicitly define T=x+y and use 2T≥B.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider a 3×3 checkerboard with black corners. There are 5 black squares. Show that at least 3 L-trominoes are needed to cover all black squares, and give an explicit placement pattern achieving 3 trominoes by listing three missing-corner choices inside the 2×2 blocks with top-left corners at (1,1), (1,2), and (2,1) or another equivalent description.
milestones_recovered.answer: Minimum is 3 trominoes for n=3.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solution gives a valid 3-tromino covering of the 5 black squares and notes 2 trominoes cannot cover 5 black squares because each covers at most 2 black squares.
hints: A lower bound is ceil(5/2)=3.
One explicit covering is: in the 2×2 block rows 1-2, cols 1-2 remove (1,2); in rows 1-2, cols 2-3 remove (1,3); in rows 2-3, cols 1-2 remove (3,1).

milestones_recovered.description: Assume n is odd and n≥5. Let the board coordinates be as usual. Show that the top two rows together with the rightmost two columns can be covered by exactly 2n−1 non-overlapping L-trominoes in such a way that every black square in that border region is covered and no tromino leaves the board. The uncovered remainder should be the (n−2)×(n−2) subboard consisting of rows 3 through n and columns 1 through n−2, which is itself an odd-by-odd checkerboard with black corners. You may describe the covering by a repeating local pattern rather than listing every tromino individually.
milestones_recovered.answer: Such a border-strip covering by 2n−1 trominoes exists, leaving an (n−2)×(n−2) odd checkerboard.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it gives a correct constructive pattern for the top two rows plus rightmost two columns, with non-overlap and exactly 2n−1 trominoes, and the leftover region is an (n−2)×(n−2) board. The exact pattern may vary.
decompose_advice: Split the border into the top 2×n strip and the remaining right-side (n−2)×2 strip, then specify a repeating tromino pattern on each part.
Check separately that the corner overlap is handled once and that all black squares in the border are covered.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the following facts, all of which must be restated in your reasoning: (i) the n×n odd checkerboard has (n^2+1)/2 black squares; (ii) each L-tromino covers either 2 black and 1 white squares or 1 black and 2 white squares, so every tromino covers at most 2 black squares; (iii) therefore any covering of all black squares needs at least ceil((n^2+1)/4) trominoes; (iv) there is an explicit 3-tromino covering for n=3; and (v) for every odd n≥5 there is a border-strip construction using 2n−1 trominoes that reduces the problem to an (n−2)×(n−2) odd board of the same kind. Using these facts, determine exactly for which odd positive integers n a covering exists, and when it exists, give the minimum number of trominoes needed.
milestones_recovered.answer: A covering exists for every odd positive integer n. The minimum number of trominoes needed is ceil((n^2+1)/4).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it concludes existence for all odd n by induction using the n=3 base case and the n→n+2 border extension, and gives the minimum as ceil((n^2+1)/4).
decompose_advice: First prove existence by induction on odd n using the 3×3 base case and the border-strip lemma.
Then solve the recurrence M(n)=M(n−2)+(2n−1) with M(3)=3, or verify directly that ceil(((n−2)^2+1)/4)+(2n−1)=ceil((n^2+1)/4).

Original problem (context):
Example 7. Investigate the convergence of the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{n+1}{3 n-1}\right)^{n^{2}}$.

Milestone:
Evaluate the limit \lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n that appears in the Cauchy root test for the series \sum_{n=1}^\infty \left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^{n^2}.

Output instruction: Put the limit value in \boxed{}.
milestones_original.note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider the series \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\) with \(a_n=\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^{n^2}\). Restate the convergence question as a problem about a standard series test suited to terms of the form \((\cdot)^{n^2}\): identify a quantity built from \(a_n\) whose limit determines convergence by the root test, and write that quantity explicitly for this \(a_n\).
milestones_recovered.answer: Use the root test with \(L=\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{a_n}\). Here \(\sqrt[n]{a_n}=\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly identifies the root test and computes \(a_n^{1/n}=((n+1)/(3n-1))^n\). ALLOW equivalent phrasing using \(\limsup\).
decompose_advice: Compute the limit of \(\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n\) by separating the base into a constant factor times a term tending to 1.
If preferred, take logarithms of \(\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n\) and reduce to limits of simpler sequences.

milestones_recovered.description:
Evaluate the limit \(L=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n\). Rewrite the base exactly as \(\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)\), so that
\[
\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n=\left(\frac13\right)^n\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)^n.
\]
Determine the limit of this product.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver concludes the limit is 0 by the stated factorization, e.g. because \((1+\frac{4}{3n-1})^n\to e^{4/3}\) while \((1/3)^n\to0\), hence the product tends to 0. ALLOW any correct equivalent argument.
decompose_advice: First prove \(\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)^n\) has a finite limit by rewriting the exponent as \(n=\frac{3n-1}{3}\cdot\frac{3n}{3n-1}\) or by logarithms.
Then combine that bounded factor with \((1/3)^n\to 0\).

milestones_recovered.description:
Prove the limit
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)^n=e^{4/3}.
\]
You may use the standard fact that if \(x_n\to 0\) and \(n x_n\to c\), then \((1+x_n)^n\to e^c\). Here take \(x_n=\frac{4}{3n-1}\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the proof correctly checks \(x_n\to0\) and \(n x_n=\frac{4n}{3n-1}\to\frac43\), then applies the standard exponential limit. ALLOW an equivalent logarithmic proof.
hints: Set \(x_n=\frac{4}{3n-1}\). Then \(x_n\to0\) and \(n x_n=\frac{4n}{3n-1}\to\frac43\).
Apply the standard limit \((1+x_n)^n\to e^{\lim n x_n}\) to get \(e^{4/3}\).

milestones_recovered.description:
Using the exact identity
\[
\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n=\left(\frac13\right)^n\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)^n,
\]
and the fact
\[
\left(1+\frac{4}{3n-1}\right)^n\to e^{4/3},
\]
compute \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response states that a sequence tending to the finite nonzero limit \(e^{4/3}\) multiplied by \((1/3)^n\to0\) gives limit 0.
hints: The second factor stays bounded and tends to \(e^{4/3}\).
Since \((1/3)^n\to0\), the whole product tends to 0.

milestones_recovered.description:
Let \(a_n=\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^{n^2}\). By the root test, if \(L=\lim_{n\to\infty}\sqrt[n]{a_n}\) exists and satisfies \(L<1\), then the series \(\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n\) converges. Here
\[
\sqrt[n]{a_n}=\left(\frac{n+1}{3n-1}\right)^n,
\]
and this limit has been found to be \(0\). Use these facts to conclude the behavior of the original series.
milestones_recovered.answer: The series \(\displaystyle \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{n+1}{3 n-1}\right)^{n^{2}}\) converges absolutely.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final conclusion is that the series converges (absolute convergence is automatic since terms are positive) by the root test with limit 0<1.
hints: Root test: limit of the \(n\)-th root equal to 0 is enough for convergence.
All terms are positive, so 'converges' and 'converges absolutely' are equivalent here.

milestones_recovered.description: Let the top of the cliff be the point A, the foot of the cliff be B, and the village be V. The cliff is sheer and has height h, so AB = h and AB is perpendicular to the ground. The village is at horizontal distance from the cliff equal to m times the cliff height, so BV = mh. The first ascetic travels from A to B and then from B to V, so his total path length is h + mh. The second ascetic rises vertically from A to a point C with AC = x, so C is directly above A, and then travels straight from C to V. Write the equality of the two travel distances as an equation involving h, m, and x, and express CV using the Pythagorean theorem.
milestones_recovered.answer: h+mh=CV with CV = √((h+x)^2+(mh)^2), so the equation is h(1+m)=√((h+x)^2+m^2h^2).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the setup defines the points and gives the equation h(1+m)=sqrt((h+x)^2+m^2 h^2), or any algebraically equivalent formulation.
decompose_advice: Define coordinates, for example B=(0,0), A=(0,h), C=(0,h+x), V=(mh,0).
Compute the first ascetic’s path and the second ascetic’s straight-line distance separately before equating them.

milestones_recovered.description: Starting from the equation h(1+m)=√((h+x)^2+m^2h^2), obtained from equal travel distances where h>0 and x is the ascent height, remove the square root and isolate x. Carry out the algebra completely.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver squares correctly and obtains x^2+2hx=h^2(2m), hence x=-h±h√(1+2m), and then selects the nonnegative root x=h(√(1+2m)-1).
decompose_advice: Square both sides first: h^2(1+m)^2=(h+x)^2+m^2h^2.
Expand both sides, cancel common terms, and solve the resulting quadratic equation in x.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the candidate height x=h(√(1+2m)-1) is physically valid in the setting where h>0 and the village is at distance mh from the cliff, so m>0. Verify both that x is nonnegative and that substituting it back into the distance equation h(1+m)=√((h+x)^2+m^2h^2) makes the equation true.
milestones_recovered.answer: For m>0, √(1+2m)>1, so x≥0; and h+x=h√(1+2m), giving √((h+x)^2+m^2h^2)=h√(1+2m+m^2)=h(1+m), so the formula is valid.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response checks nonnegativity and verifies substitution back into the original equation, or gives an equivalent validity check.
hints: Then the expression under the square root becomes h^2[(1+2m)+m^2]=h^2(1+m)^2.

milestones_recovered.description: The travel lengths are equal. The first ascetic’s path length is h+mh=h(1+m). The second ascetic rises to height x above the cliff top and then goes straight to the village, giving the equation h(1+m)=√((h+x)^2+m^2h^2). Solving this equation gives x=h(√(1+2m)-1), and this value satisfies the original distance condition. State the requested height to which the second ascetic rose.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final stated height is exactly x=h(√(1+2m)-1), with equivalent notation allowed.
hints: The final answer should be the ascent above the cliff top, not the total height above the ground.
No further derivation is needed beyond stating the solved value for x.

$1 \cdot 5 n$ is a fixed positive integer, find the sum of all positive integers that have the following property: in binary, this number has exactly $2 n$ digits, of which $n$ are 1s and $n$ are 0s (the leading digit cannot be $0$).

Milestone:
Fix any non-leading bit position in a length-2n valid binary number: the first bit is 1, the chosen bit is also required to be 1, and the whole string must contain exactly n ones and n zeros. Compute how many such valid binary strings there are.

Output instruction: Put the counting expression in \boxed{}.
milestones_original.note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description: Let S be the set of all positive integers whose binary expansion has exactly 2n digits, exactly n of those digits are 1, exactly n are 0, and the leading digit is 1. Write the sum of all numbers in S as a sum of place-value contributions. Specifically, for each binary position k from 0 to 2n-1 (where position 0 is the least significant bit), express the total contribution of position k as 2^k times the number of valid binary strings in S having a 1 in position k.
milestones_recovered.answer: If N_k is the number of valid 2n-digit binary strings with n ones, n zeros, leading digit 1, and bit k equal to 1, then the required sum is \(\sum_{k=0}^{2n-1} 2^k N_k\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer defines N_k correctly and writes the total sum as a place-value sum over k. ALLOW equivalent indexing of bits.
decompose_advice: Split into the case k=2n-1 (the leading bit) and the cases 0<=k<=2n-2.
For each case, count how many valid strings have bit k equal to 1 by choosing positions of the remaining ones.

milestones_recovered.description: Count the number of valid binary strings of length 2n with exactly n ones and n zeros, under the condition that the leading bit (position 2n-1) is 1. Determine how many such strings have the leading bit equal to 1; this is exactly the number of valid strings in the original problem.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF they choose the remaining n-1 ones among the remaining 2n-1 positions. ALLOW equivalent forms such as \(\binom{2n-1}{n}\).
hints: The leading bit is already forced to be 1, so only n-1 more ones must be placed.
Choose the positions of those remaining ones among the other 2n-1 bit positions.

milestones_recovered.description: Fix a non-leading position k with 0 <= k <= 2n-2. Count the number N_k of binary strings of length 2n that have exactly n ones and n zeros, have leading bit 1, and also have bit k equal to 1. Give N_k in closed form.
milestones_recovered.answer: For every \(0\le k\le 2n-2\), \(N_k=\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF they argue that two 1s are fixed (the leading bit and bit k), then choose the remaining n-2 ones among the remaining 2n-2 positions.
hints: After fixing the leading bit and bit k to be 1, there are n-2 more ones left to place.
Those n-2 ones can be chosen freely among the remaining 2n-2 positions.

milestones_recovered.description: Prove the combinatorial identity \(\binom{2n-1}{n-1}=2\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\), and then simplify the difference \(\binom{2n-1}{n-1}-\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final simplified difference is correct. ALLOW use of Pascal's identity directly; the extra displayed form need not be repeated exactly.
hints: Use Pascal's identity: \(\binom{m}{r}=\binom{m-1}{r-1}+\binom{m-1}{r}\).

milestones_recovered.description:
Using the facts that the leading position contributes \(2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-1}{n-1}\) to the total sum and each non-leading position contributes \(2^k\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\) for 0\le k\le 2n-2, simplify the full sum
\[
\sum_{x\in S} x=2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-1}{n-1}+\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\sum_{k=0}^{2n-2}2^k.
\]
Evaluate the geometric sum and reduce the expression to a compact closed form.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the geometric series is evaluated correctly and substituted correctly. ALLOW any algebraically equivalent form.
decompose_advice: Then substitute the values of \(N_{2n-1}\) and \(N_k\) into the place-value sum from the model milestone.

milestones_recovered.description:
Starting from
\[
(2^{2n}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-1}{n-1},
\]
use the identity \(\binom{2n-1}{n-1}-\binom{2n-2}{n-2}=\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) to rewrite the sum in the form
\[
(2^{2n}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-2}{n-1}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-2}{n-2}.
\]
Then combine the first and third terms.
milestones_recovered.answer: The sum becomes \(2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-1}{n-1}-\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-2}{n-1}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\), hence after combining the terms with \(\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\) it simplifies toward \((2^{2n}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) using symmetry.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF they correctly introduce \(\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) via Pascal's identity and move toward the final symmetric binomial form. ALLOW equivalent algebraic rearrangements.
decompose_advice: Replace \(\binom{2n-1}{n-1}\) by \(\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\).
Use the symmetry \(\binom{2n-2}{n-2}=\binom{2n-2}{n}\) only if needed, but the cleanest route is to collect coefficients first.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the closed-form candidate \((2^{2n}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) against the smallest case n=1. For n=1, list all valid binary numbers of length 2 with one 1 and one 0 and leading digit 1, compute their sum directly, and compare.
milestones_recovered.answer: For n=1 the only valid binary number is 10_2=2, and \((2^{2}-1)\binom{0}{0}=3\cdot1\) is not 2, so the candidate must instead simplify to the correct final form obtained by direct algebra from the computed expression.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF they detect the mismatch for n=1. This is a checkpoint to prevent accepting an incorrect simplification.
hints: The only 2-digit binary string with one 1, one 0, and leading digit 1 is 10.
Any proposed final formula must equal 2 when n=1.

milestones_recovered.description:
Simplify correctly the expression
\[
(2^{2n}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+2^{2n-1}\binom{2n-1}{n-1}
\]
into a single binomial-coefficient form. Use Pascal's identity \(\binom{2n-1}{n-1}=\binom{2n-2}{n-2}+\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) and the ratio
\[
\binom{2n-2}{n-1}=\frac{n}{n-1}\binom{2n-2}{n-2}
\]
for n>=2, or an equivalent correct algebraic method. The goal is a closed form valid for all positive integers n.
milestones_recovered.answer: \(\sum_{x\in S} x=\binom{2n-2}{n-2}(2^{2n-1}(2n-1)-1)/(n-1)\) is one possible intermediate form for n\ge2, equivalently \(\sum_{x\in S}=\binom{2n-1}{n-1}2^{2n-1}+(2^{2n-1}-1)\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver preserves the correct expression from earlier and does not replace it by a false simplification. ALLOW the unsimplified but correct closed form. For n=1, the separate value 2 is valid.
decompose_advice: First substitute Pascal's identity into the second term.
Then collect the coefficients of \(\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\) and \(\binom{2n-2}{n-1}\) carefully, checking n=1 afterward.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the full solution from these established facts: (i) the total sum is \(\sum_{k=0}^{2n-1}2^kN_k\); (ii) for the leading bit, \(N_{2n-1}=\binom{2n-1}{n-1}\); (iii) for each non-leading bit k with 0\le k\le 2n-2, \(N_k=\binom{2n-2}{n-2}\); and (iv) \(\sum_{k=0}^{2n-2}2^k=2^{2n-1}-1\). Combine them to state the final sum of all such integers.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer matches this formula or an algebraically equivalent correct form, with the n=1 case included automatically.
hints: Write the leading-bit contribution separately from the other bits.
There are 2n-1 non-leading positions, but their weights are different, so sum the powers of 2 explicitly.

## Problem 4 (Variant 131).

When studying the operation of a new type of heat engine working cyclically, it was found that for part of the period it receives heat, with the absolute value of the heat supply power expressed by the law: $P_{1}(t)=P_{0} \frac{\sin \omega t}{100+\sin t^{2}}, 0<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega}$. The gas performs work, developing mechanical power $P_{2}(t)=3 P_{0} \frac{\sin (2 \omega t)}{100+\sin (2 t)^{2}}, 0<t<\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}$. The work done on the gas by external bodies is $2 / 3$ of the magnitude of the work done by the gas. Determine the efficiency of the engine.

## Problem 4 (Variant 131).

When studying the operation of a new type of heat engine working cyclically, it was found that for part of the period it receives heat, with the absolute value of the heat supply power expressed by the law: $P_{1}(t)=P_{0} \frac{\sin \omega t}{100+\sin t^{2}}, 0<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega}$. The gas performs work, developing mechanical power $P_{2}(t)=3 P_{0} \frac{\sin (2 \omega t)}{100+\sin (2 t)^{2}}, 0<t<\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}$. The work done on the gas by external bodies is $2 / 3$ of the magnitude of the work done by the gas. Determine the efficiency of the engine.

Milestone:
If P_2(t)=aP_1(bt) for positive constants a,b, then the positive work of the gas is A_+=\int P_2(t)dt and the received heat is Q_+=\int P_1(t)dt over the stated positive intervals. Output the resulting relation between A_+ and Q_+ for the specific case a=3, b=2.

Output instruction: Put the relation between A_+ and Q_+ in \boxed{}.
milestones_original.note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description:
Let the heat absorbed by the engine during the part of the cycle where it receives heat be Q_in, and let the mechanical work done by the gas during the part of the cycle where it develops power be W_gas. Using power = rate of energy transfer, write Q_in and W_gas as definite integrals from the given power laws
P_1(t)=P_0 * sin(omega t)/(100+sin^2 t) for 0<t<pi/omega,
and
P_2(t)=3P_0 * sin(2 omega t)/(100+sin^2(2t)) for 0<t<pi/(2 omega).
Also express the engine efficiency eta in terms of the net work per cycle and Q_in, given that the work done on the gas by external bodies has magnitude equal to (2/3) of W_gas.
milestones_recovered.answer: Q_in=\int_0^{\pi/\omega} P_0\,\frac{\sin(\omega t)}{100+\sin^2 t}\,dt,\quad W_gas=\int_0^{\pi/(2\omega)} 3P_0\,\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{100+\sin^2(2t)}\,dt,\quad \eta=\frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}},\; W_{net}=W_{gas}-\frac23 W_{gas}=\frac13 W_{gas}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the two integrals are written correctly and efficiency is given as eta=(1/3)W_gas/Q_in or equivalent.
decompose_advice: Next isolate each integral and compare them by substitutions.
Be explicit that the external work is opposite in sign to the gas work, so net work is their difference in magnitude.

milestones_recovered.description:
Show that the two energy integrals are proportional by direct substitution. Start from
Q_in=\int_0^{\pi/\omega} P_0\,\frac{\sin(\omega t)}{100+\sin^2 t}\,dt
and
W_gas=\int_0^{\pi/(2\omega)} 3P_0\,\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{100+\sin^2(2t)}\,dt.
In the second integral, use the substitution u=2t, so dt=du/2, and transform the limits exactly. Then compare the resulting integral with Q_in.
milestones_recovered.answer: With u=2t,\; W_gas=\frac{3P_0}{2}\int_0^{\pi/\omega}\frac{\sin(\omega u)}{100+\sin^2 u}\,du=\frac32\,Q_in.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the substitution u=2t is done correctly, limits become 0 to pi/omega, and the conclusion W_gas=(3/2)Q_in is obtained.
hints: Because sin(2\omega t)=sin(\omega(2t)) and sin^2(2t)=sin^2 u after u=2t, the integrands match exactly.
Do not try to evaluate the integral numerically; only compare forms.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the facts that the work done on the gas by external bodies has magnitude (2/3)W_gas, the net work per cycle is W_net=W_gas-(2/3)W_gas=(1/3)W_gas, and from the transformed integrals one has W_gas=(3/2)Q_in. Compute the efficiency eta=W_net/Q_in.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF eta=(1/3)*(3/2)=1/2 or 50% is obtained.
hints: First compute W_net=(1/3)W_gas.
Then divide by Q_in and substitute W_gas=(3/2)Q_in.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the computed efficiency is physically reasonable using only the facts Q_in>0 on 0<t<pi/omega because sin(omega t)>0 there and the denominator 100+sin^2 t is positive, and W_net=(1/3)W_gas with W_gas=(3/2)Q_in. Verify that 0<eta<1.
milestones_recovered.answer: Since Q_in>0 and W_gas=(3/2)Q_in>0, we get W_net=(1/3)W_gas=(1/2)Q_in, so 0<eta=W_net/Q_in=1/2<1.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response confirms positivity and that 1/2 lies between 0 and 1.
hints: On the stated interval, omega t runs from 0 to pi, so sin(omega t) is positive except at endpoints.
A heat-engine efficiency must be less than 1, and 1/2 passes this check.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the result: the absorbed heat is Q_in=\int_0^{\pi/\omega} P_0\,\frac{\sin(\omega t)}{100+\sin^2 t}\,dt, the gas work is W_gas=\int_0^{\pi/(2\omega)} 3P_0\,\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{100+\sin^2(2t)}\,dt, the substitution u=2t gives W_gas=(3/2)Q_in, and the net work is W_net=W_gas-(2/3)W_gas=(1/3)W_gas. State the engine efficiency exactly.
milestones_recovered.answer: \eta=\frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}=\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{W_{gas}}{Q_{in}}=\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{2}=50\%.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer is exactly 1/2 or 50%, with no new derivation beyond assembling the stated facts.
hints: Combine the two already established identities W_gas=(3/2)Q_in and W_net=(1/3)W_gas.
Then eta=W_net/Q_in.
