milestones_recovered.description: Model the shop as a 28×28 table whose rows are the 28 sellers and whose columns are the 28 fish kinds. For each cell (seller, kind), define its type to be either 0 (Mediterranean) or 1 (Black Sea). A person’s purchase can be represented by choosing exactly one cell in each row and exactly one cell in each column, so each person corresponds to a permutation σ of the 28 fish kinds, where seller i gives that person fish kind σ(i). Define the person’s 28-bit type word w by w_i = type of the fish bought from seller i. The condition in the problem says that for any two distinct people, their 28-bit words are different. Explain why the number k of people is at most the number of distinct 28-bit words realizable by such permutation selections.
milestones_recovered.answer: Each person corresponds to a permutation σ, and two different people must have different 28-bit words w; hence k is at most the number of distinct words w obtainable from permutation selections.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response formalizes people as permutation selections and states that pairwise type-difference means distinct binary words, so counting people reduces to counting realizable words.
decompose_advice: Explicitly define why 'one fish from each seller' and 'one fish of each kind' is equivalent to a permutation.
Separate the encoding step: first encode shop entries as 0/1, then encode each person by the 28-bit word read along the chosen permutation.

milestones_recovered.description: Fix any 28×28 table A=(a_{ij}) with entries in {0,1}, where i is the seller index and j is the fish-kind index. For a permutation σ of {1,2,…,28}, define its word f(σ)=(a_{1,σ(1)},a_{2,σ(2)},…,a_{28,σ(28)}). Prove the following counting bound: the number of distinct words f(σ) over all permutations σ is at most 2^{27}. A useful route is to compare f(σ) with f(τ∘σ), where τ is any permutation of fish kinds, and especially to study what happens when τ is a transposition swapping two fish kinds.
milestones_recovered.answer: For every fixed table A, the set {f(σ): σ permutation} has size at most 2^{27}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the milestone states the correct transformed target: show at most 2^27 distinct binary words arise from all permutation diagonals. The proof itself may be deferred.
decompose_advice: First prove an invariance/parity relation for words obtained from two permutations differing by a transposition of fish kinds.
Then deduce that all realizable words lie in one of two parity classes of {0,1}^{28}, giving the 2^27 bound.

milestones_recovered.description: Let A=(a_{ij}) be any n×n matrix with entries in {0,1}. For a permutation σ of {1,2,…,n}, define S(σ)=a_{1,σ(1)}+a_{2,σ(2)}+⋯+a_{n,σ(n)} modulo 2. Prove that the parity of S(σ) depends only on the parity of the permutation σ: more precisely, there exist constants C,D in {0,1} depending only on A such that S(σ) ≡ C + D·sgn_par(σ) mod 2, where sgn_par(σ)=0 for even σ and 1 for odd σ. Equivalently, if two permutations σ and π have the same parity, then S(σ)≡S(π) mod 2. You may use and prove the transposition step: if τ swaps columns p and q, then S(τ∘σ)-S(σ) ≡ a_{r,q}+a_{s,p}-a_{r,p}-a_{s,q} mod 2 for the two affected rows r,s, which modulo 2 equals a fixed value independent of σ except for changing permutation parity once.
milestones_recovered.answer: There is a fixed bit E such that for every transposition τ and every permutation σ, S(τ∘σ) ≡ S(σ)+E mod 2; therefore S(σ) is determined by the parity class of σ, so at most two values of S occur, one on even permutations and one on odd permutations.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response proves that S(σ) mod 2 is constant on each permutation parity class, or equivalently affine-linear in permutation parity. Minor variations in notation are fine.
decompose_advice: Isolate the case where two permutations differ by one transposition and compute how S changes mod 2.
Then use the fact that permutations with the same parity are connected by an even number of transpositions.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume A=(a_{ij}) is a 28×28 {0,1}-matrix. For each permutation σ, define the 28-bit word f(σ)=(a_{1,σ(1)},…,a_{28,σ(28)}). Also define the bit-sum parity P(f(σ))=a_{1,σ(1)}+⋯+a_{28,σ(28)} modulo 2. Using the fact that P(f(σ)) is constant on all even permutations and constant on all odd permutations, prove that every realizable word f(σ) belongs to at most two parity classes of {0,1}^{28}: one fixed parity for even σ and one fixed parity for odd σ. Conclude that the total number of distinct realizable words is at most 2^{27}.
milestones_recovered.answer: All words from even permutations have one fixed bit-sum parity, and all words from odd permutations have one fixed bit-sum parity; each parity class in {0,1}^{28} has size 2^{27}, so the union of the realizable sets has size at most 2^{27}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly concludes the 2^27 upper bound from the parity lemma. Best answer notices that the two fixed parities may coincide, so the bound remains at most 2^27.
hints: A subset of {0,1}^{28} in which every word has fixed coordinate-sum parity contains at most half of all 2^{28} words.

If even and odd permutations both produce words of the same parity, the bound is even smaller; if they produce opposite parities, still only one parity class can occur for each side, so total distinct words is at most 2^{27} once the transposition relation is tracked correctly.

milestones_recovered.description: Construct an explicit 28×28 shop arrangement that allows 2^{27} people. One convenient target is to define the cell type in row i and column j by a_{ij}=x_i+y_j modulo 2 for suitable row bits x_i and column bits y_j. Show that for such a table, every permutation σ produces a word f(σ)=(x_1+y_{σ(1)},…,x_{28}+y_{σ(28)}) whose total parity is fixed, and then choose x_i,y_j so that every 28-bit word of that fixed parity is realized by some permutation. Give the construction and prove it realizes exactly 2^{27} distinct words.
milestones_recovered.answer: Take a_{ij}=1 if and only if i+j is even (equivalently a_{ij}=r_i+c_j mod 2 with r_i=i mod 2 and c_j=1). Then along any permutation σ, the word entries are a_{i,σ(i)}=r_i+c_{σ(i)} mod 2, and since {σ(1),…,σ(28)} is all columns, the bit-sum parity is fixed. Moreover for any 28-bit word of that parity, choosing columns with prescribed c_{σ(i)} realizes it, so exactly 2^{27} distinct words occur.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF an explicit construction is given and it is proved that exactly half of all 28-bit words are realizable. Any equivalent rank-1 over F2 construction is fine.
decompose_advice: First verify the construction respects the shop rules: each seller has one fish of each kind, each cell has one of two types.
Then prove surjectivity onto one parity class by solving for a permutation that matches a target word.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the explicit construction from the previous milestone on a smaller analogous case n=2 or n=4. For n=2, write down the 2×2 table a_{ij}=i+j mod 2 (or any equivalent parity-based table), list all permutation words, and verify that exactly 2^{n-1} distinct words occur. Explain why this is consistent with the general formula 2^{27} when n=28.
milestones_recovered.answer: In the small case, the realizable words are exactly half of all binary words, confirming the general pattern 'maximum = 2^{n-1}', hence for n=28 the candidate maximum is 2^{27}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response performs a correct small-case check showing half the words occur. This is only a checkpoint, not the main proof.
hints: For n=2 there are two permutations; compute the two words directly.
Observe that the two resulting words have the same bit-sum parity.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the solution. Use these established facts: (1) each person corresponds to a permutation σ and thus to a 28-bit word f(σ), and different people must have different words; therefore k is at most the number of distinct realizable words. (2) For any 28×28 binary shop table, all realizable words lie in at most one parity class of {0,1}^{28}, so there are at most 2^{27} distinct realizable words. (3) There exists an explicit table for which exactly 2^{27} distinct realizable words occur. Conclude the maximum possible value of k.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final conclusion is exactly k_max = 2^27, justified by combining the upper bound and the matching construction.

milestones_recovered.description: Prove the following standalone claim. Let x1<x2<x3<x4<x5 be five distinct numbers chosen from S={a+b,a+c,a+d,b+c,b+d,c+d}, and suppose they form an arithmetic progression. Then among these five numbers there exist three terms u<v<w with v-u=w-v such that either (i) u,v,w are all of the form t+u1,t+u2,t+u3 for one fixed t in {a,b,c,d}, or (ii) after subtracting a common fixed number from u,v,w, one obtains three distinct elements of {a,b,c,d} in arithmetic progression. milestones_recovered.answer: Any 5-term arithmetic progression contains three equally spaced terms x1,x3,x5 and also x2,x3,x4. In either case, equality of consecutive differences among those three sums implies equality of consecutive differences among three elements of {a,b,c,d}, so a,b,c,d contain a 3-term arithmetic progression. Therefore S cannot contain a 5-term arithmetic progression if {a,b,c,d} has no 3-term arithmetic progression.

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose three three-digit numbers A, B, C are formed from the digits 1 through 9 exactly once, with no zero digit. Prove that if A·B·C is divisible by 5^4, then the digit 5 must be the units digit of one of the numbers, and that number must be divisible by 125. Also determine all possible three-digit numbers with non-zero distinct digits, using digits from 1 through 9, that are divisible by 125.
milestones_recovered.answer: A factor 5 can only come from a number ending in 5, since no number can end in 0. Because only one digit 5 exists, exactly one of A,B,C can contribute factors of 5, so that number must contribute all four factors of 5; hence it must be divisible by 125. The only possible such three-digit number with non-zero distinct digits is 375.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it states that the unique digit 5 must sit in the units place of the unique multiple of 5, and that this number must be divisible by 625 or at least supply 5^4 via divisibility by 125 together with another factor 5 from itself; but the correct conclusion here is that a three-digit number ending in 5 can have at most 5^4 only if divisible by 625, impossible except 625 uses 5 once? Better criterion for verifier: accept the concrete valid outcome that the only candidate source of four 5s among 3-digit numbers with one digit 5 and no zero is 375 because 375 = 3·5^4. ALLOW any proof by checking 125, 250, 375, 500, 625, 750, 875 and rejecting those with 0, repeated 5, or forbidden digits.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume the previous divisibility analysis has shown that one of the numbers must be 375, since 375 = 3·5^4 and it is the only three-digit number with non-zero distinct digits from 1 through 9 that can supply the required four factors of 5. The remaining digits are 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 9, to be arranged into two three-digit numbers X and Y. Show that to make 375·X·Y divisible by 2^4, it is enough and necessary that X·Y be divisible by 16. Reformulate the problem as: partition the digits 1,2,4,6,8,9 into two three-digit numbers whose product is divisible by 16.
milestones_recovered.answer: Since 375 has no factor of 2, the entire factor 2^4 must come from X·Y. So the task reduces to partitioning 1,2,4,6,8,9 into two three-digit numbers X,Y with 16 | X·Y.

milestones_recovered.description: Let X and Y be three-digit numbers formed from the digits 1,2,4,6,8,9 exactly once. Characterize when X·Y is divisible by 16 using decimal divisibility facts. In particular, use that for any integer n, divisibility by 2, 4, and 8 depends only on the last 1, 2, and 3 digits respectively, and determine a practical criterion from the units and tens digits of X and Y that guarantees v2(XY) = v2(X)+v2(Y) is at least 4.
milestones_recovered.answer: At least one of X,Y must be even. More precisely, X·Y is divisible by 16 if the two numbers together contribute four factors of 2, which can be checked from their endings: a number ending in 2 or 6 contributes exactly one factor of 2 unless its last two digits are divisible by 4; a number ending in 4 or 8 contributes at least two factors of 2, and sometimes three if its last three digits are divisible by 8. A practical route is to construct endings so one number is divisible by 8 and the other is even, or both are divisible by 4.

milestones_recovered.description: Let e_t be Applejack’s energy at the start of minute t for t=1,2,\dots,60, with e_1=100. In each minute she chooses one of two actions: buck, which adds e_t to the total tree count and changes the next energy to e_{t+1}=e_t-1; or rest, which adds 0 trees and changes the next energy to e_{t+1}=e_t+1. Let B be the set of minutes on which she bucks and let R be the set of minutes on which she rests, so |B|+|R|=60. Write a formula for the total number of trees T in terms of the starting energies on the bucking minutes, and write the final energy e_{61} in terms of |B| and |R|.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly models buck/rest and gives both formulas; equivalent notation is fine.
decompose_advice: Express the total trees as a sum over exactly the bucking minutes.
Track net energy change after 60 minutes using +1 for each rest and -1 for each buck.

milestones_recovered.description: Fix a nonnegative integer b, the number of bucking minutes in the 60-minute process. Then there are 60-b resting minutes. For a fixed b, determine the arrangement of the 60 actions that maximizes the total number of trees. Your answer must explicitly identify the optimal order of the actions and the starting energies of the b bucking minutes in that order.
milestones_recovered.answer: For fixed b, the maximum occurs when all 60-b rests happen first and then all b bucks happen. In that order, the starting energies on the bucking minutes are 100+(60-b), 100+(60-b)-1, \dots, 100+(60-b)-(b-1), i.e. 160-b, 159-b, \dots, 161-2b.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it states that all rests should come before all bucks for fixed b, with a correct exchange argument or equivalent, and lists the resulting buck energies. Minor indexing variants are fine.
decompose_advice: Compare two adjacent minutes where a buck is followed by a rest versus a rest followed by a buck, and compute which gives more trees.
After proving every rest should be moved earlier, list the energy values during the final consecutive bucking block.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume the actions are ordered optimally for a fixed number b of bucking minutes: first 60-b rests, then b bucks. Compute the total number of trees as a function T(b). Then maximize T(b) over all integers b with 0\le b\le 60.
milestones_recovered.answer: T(b)=\sum_{k=0}^{b-1}(160-b-k)=\frac{b(321-3b)}{2}. This quadratic is maximized at b=53 or b=54, and T(53)=T(54)=4770.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response derives an equivalent quadratic formula for T(b) and finds the maximum value 4770 attained at b=53 and 54.
decompose_advice: Sum the arithmetic sequence 160-b,159-b,\dots,161-2b.
Treat T(b) as a concave quadratic in b and check the nearest integers to its vertex, or compare consecutive values T(b+1)-T(b).

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the candidate optimal schedules with 53 or 54 bucking minutes are feasible and achieve the claimed total. Use the schedule structure 'all rests first, then all bucks.' Compute the ending energy in each case and confirm it never becomes negative during the bucking block.
milestones_recovered.answer: For b=53: rest 7 times to reach energy 107, then buck 53 times from energies 107 down to 55, ending at 54; total = 53(107+55)/2 = 4770. For b=54: rest 6 times to reach energy 106, then buck 54 times from energies 106 down to 53, ending at 52; total = 54(106+53)/2 = 4770. Both are feasible.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it verifies at least one maximizing schedule correctly, and preferably both b=53 and b=54, with nonnegative energies and total 4770.
hints: After r rests, the energy becomes 100+r before the bucking block starts.
A consecutive block of b bucks decreases energy by exactly b, so the starting energies form a descending arithmetic sequence.

milestones_recovered.description: Let (x_n)_{n\ge 1} be a sequence of nonzero real numbers satisfying x_n=\dfrac{x_{n-2}x_{n-1}}{2x_{n-2}-x_{n-1}} for every integer n>2, and suppose every denominator 2x_{n-2}-x_{n-1} is nonzero so the recurrence is defined. Rewrite the recurrence in a simpler form by introducing y_n=1/x_n, and state the transformed recurrence and the final goal in terms of y_n. The final goal is to determine exactly for which initial pair (x_1,x_2) the original sequence has infinitely many integer terms.
milestones_recovered.answer: With y_n=1/x_n, the recurrence becomes y_n=2y_{n-1}-y_{n-2} for n>2. Equivalently, (y_n) is an arithmetic progression: y_n=y_1+(n-1)(y_2-y_1). The problem becomes: determine for which nonzero reals y_1,y_2 the reciprocals x_n=1/y_n contain infinitely many integers.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer correctly derives 1/x_n=(2x_{n-2}-x_{n-1})/(x_{n-2}x_{n-1})=2/x_{n-1}-1/x_{n-2}, hence y_n=2y_{n-1}-y_{n-2}, and restates the goal in y-variables.
decompose_advice: First compute 1/x_n explicitly from the given recurrence.
Then identify that y_n- y_{n-1} is constant, so y_n is arithmetic.

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose a real sequence (y_n) satisfies y_n=2y_{n-1}-y_{n-2} for every integer n>2. Prove directly that the differences y_n-y_{n-1} are constant, and deduce an explicit formula for y_n in terms of y_1 and y_2.
milestones_recovered.answer: For n>2, y_n-y_{n-1}=y_{n-1}-y_{n-2}, so all consecutive differences are equal to d:=y_2-y_1. Hence y_n=y_1+(n-1)d=y_1+(n-1)(y_2-y_1).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the proof shows constant first difference and concludes the stated explicit formula.
hints: Subtract y_{n-1} from both sides of y_n=2y_{n-1}-y_{n-2}.
A sequence with constant successive difference d has form y_n=y_1+(n-1)d.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the facts that y_n=1/x_n and y_n=y_1+(n-1)(y_2-y_1), write x_n explicitly in terms of a:=1/x_1 and b:=1/x_2. Then split the problem into the two cases b-a=0 and b-a\ne 0.
milestones_recovered.answer: If a=1/x_1 and b=1/x_2, then y_n=a+(n-1)(b-a), so x_n=\dfrac{1}{a+(n-1)(b-a)}. If b-a=0, then x_n is constant. If b-a\ne 0, then x_n is the reciprocal of a nonconstant arithmetic progression.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the explicit formula x_n=1/(a+(n-1)(b-a)) is obtained and the constant/nonconstant dichotomy is stated.
decompose_advice: Substitute y_1=a and y_2=b into the arithmetic-progression formula.
Treat separately the common-difference-zero and common-difference-nonzero cases.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume x_n=\dfrac{1}{a+(n-1)d} for all n\ge 1, where a and d are real numbers, a\ne 0, and d\ne 0. Determine all possibilities for which the sequence (x_n) contains infinitely many integer terms. Your argument must use only the explicit formula and the fact that if m is an integer then either |m|\ge 1 or m=0.
milestones_recovered.answer: There are no such possibilities when d\ne 0. Indeed, since a+(n-1)d is a nonconstant arithmetic progression, |a+(n-1)d|\to\infty as n\to\infty, so |x_n|=1/|a+(n-1)d|\to 0. Therefore for all sufficiently large n, |x_n|<1. An integer with absolute value less than 1 must be 0, but x_n\ne 0 for all n, so only finitely many terms can be integers.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it concludes that nonconstant case gives only finitely many integer terms because x_n tends to 0 but never equals 0. Equivalent arguments using eventual boundedness by 1 are fine.
hints: A nonconstant arithmetic progression a+(n-1)d has unbounded absolute value as n grows.
If |x_n|<1 and x_n is an integer, then x_n must be 0, impossible because x_n is nonzero.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume x_1=x_2=c for some nonzero real number c, and the recurrence is x_n=\dfrac{x_{n-2}x_{n-1}}{2x_{n-2}-x_{n-1}} for n>2. Determine exactly when the sequence contains infinitely many integer terms.
milestones_recovered.answer: If x_1=x_2=c, then every term equals c, so the sequence contains infinitely many integer terms exactly when c is an integer and c\ne 0.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it shows the sequence is constant when the first two terms are equal, and then identifies nonzero integer constants as exactly those giving infinitely many integer terms.
hints: Substitute x_{n-2}=x_{n-1}=c into the recurrence.
A constant sequence has infinitely many integer terms iff its constant value is an integer.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that if x_1=x_2=c with c\ne 0, then the recurrence denominator 2x_{n-2}-x_{n-1} never vanishes, and if x_1=x_2=c with c=0 the original problem's nonzero hypothesis fails. State the consequence for the final answer.
milestones_recovered.answer: For the constant sequence x_n=c\ne 0, the denominator is 2c-c=c\ne 0, so the recurrence is valid. The case c=0 is excluded because all terms are required to be nonzero. Thus only nonzero integer constants are admissible.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF it verifies denominator nonvanishing for c\ne 0 and excludes c=0 by the problem statement.
hints: In the constant case the denominator simplifies to c.
The problem assumes every x_n is nonzero.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the following established facts: (1) with y_n=1/x_n, one gets y_n=2y_{n-1}-y_{n-2}; (2) therefore y_n=y_1+(n-1)(y_2-y_1), so x_n=1/(a+(n-1)d) where a=1/x_1 and d=1/x_2-1/x_1; (3) if d\ne 0, then x_n\to 0 and since all x_n are nonzero, only finitely many x_n can be integers; (4) if d=0, then x_1=x_2=c and the sequence is constantly c, which has infinitely many integer terms exactly when c is a nonzero integer. Combine these facts to answer the original problem: for which pairs (x_1,x_2) does the sequence contain infinitely many integral terms?
milestones_recovered.answer: Exactly for pairs with x_1=x_2=k, where k is a nonzero integer.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final set of initial pairs is precisely {(k,k): k\in\mathbb Z, k\ne 0}.
hints: The condition d=0 is equivalent to 1/x_2=1/x_1, hence x_1=x_2 because the terms are nonzero.
Nonconstant case gives only finitely many integers; constant case gives infinitely many iff the constant is a nonzero integer.

Milestone:
Let the two projection planes be perpendicular, with their intersection line through A. In the second projection plane, triangle AB_2C_2 is equilateral with side 1, so B_2C_2=1 and AC_2=1. milestones_recovered.description: Let plane P and plane Q be two mutually perpendicular planes. Let the orthogonal projection of triangle ABC onto P be triangle A_P B_P C_P, and onto Q be triangle A_Q B_Q C_Q. The problem states that both projected triangles are equilateral with side length 1, and that the actual edge length AB equals sqrt(5)/2. Introduce coordinates adapted to the two perpendicular planes by taking P to be the xy-plane and Q to be the xz-plane. For any segment, its squared length in space equals the sum of the squared lengths of its projections onto P and Q minus the square of its x-coordinate difference, because |UV|^2=(Δx)^2+(Δy)^2+(Δz)^2, while |U_PV_P|^2=(Δx)^2+(Δy)^2 and |U_QV_Q|^2=(Δx)^2+(Δz)^2. Using this setup, express the constraints on the coordinate differences for AB, BC, CA and restate the goal as determining the other two side lengths BC and CA and then the perimeter.
milestones_recovered.answer: For each side UV in {AB,BC,CA}, the projections satisfy |U_PV_P|=1 and |U_QV_Q|=1, so (Δx_{UV})^2+(Δy_{UV})^2=1 and (Δx_{UV})^2+(Δz_{UV})^2=1. Hence |UV|^2=2-(Δx_{UV})^2. Since AB=sqrt(5)/2, we get (Δx_{AB})^2=2-5/4=3/4. The goal is to determine (Δx_{BC})^2 and (Δx_{CA})^2 from the equilateral-triangle structure of both projections, then compute BC and CA and add the three side lengths.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly introduces coordinates with P: z=0 and Q: y=0 (or an equivalent orthogonal setup), states |UV|^2=|U_PV_P|^2+|U_QV_Q|^2-(shared-axis difference)^2, specializes to |UV|^2=2-(Δx_{UV})^2, and identifies the need to find the x-differences for the other two sides.
decompose_advice: Make the coordinate identity explicit by writing all three formulas for a generic segment UV.
Then isolate the numerical consequence for AB from AB=sqrt(5)/2.

milestones_recovered.description: In an equilateral triangle of side length 1 lying in a plane, choose oriented edge vectors u, v, w that go around the triangle consecutively, so u+v+w=0 and |u|=|v|=|w|=1. Prove that for any fixed unit vector e in that plane, if a=u·e, b=v·e, c=w·e are the scalar components of the three edge vectors along e, then a+b+c=0 and a^2+b^2+c^2=3/2. Also deduce that if one of a^2,b^2,c^2 equals 3/4, then the other two both equal 3/8.
milestones_recovered.answer: The components satisfy a+b+c=(u+v+w)·e=0. Also u,v,w make angles differing by 120°, so relative to e their components are cos θ, cos(θ+120°), cos(θ+240°), whose squares sum to 3/2. Therefore if one square is 3/4, the other two squares sum to 3/4 and, by symmetry of the two remaining values when one direction is fixed up to sign, they are both 3/8.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response proves a+b+c=0 and a^2+b^2+c^2=3/2, either via explicit cosine identities or by rotating coordinates. For the last deduction, ACCEPT any correct derivation yielding the pair 3/8 and 3/8 from total 3/2 and one value 3/4.
decompose_advice: First prove the generic identities a+b+c=0 and a^2+b^2+c^2=3/2 for three unit vectors 120° apart.
Then substitute one value 3/4 and solve for the remaining two squared values.

milestones_recovered.description: Apply the previous component lemma independently to each projection plane, but restate all needed facts here so the task is standalone. In the xy-plane, the projected triangle A_P B_P C_P is equilateral of side 1. Let the oriented edge vectors around this projected triangle be p_AB, p_BC, p_CA. Their x-components, denoted x_AB, x_BC, x_CA, satisfy x_AB+x_BC+x_CA=0 and x_AB^2+x_BC^2+x_CA^2=3/2. The same x-components appear for the xz-plane projection because orthogonal projection onto the xz-plane preserves the x-coordinate difference of each side. Since the actual side formula is |UV|^2=2-x_{UV}^2 and AB=sqrt(5)/2, we know x_AB^2=3/4. Use the equilateral projection component identities to determine x_BC^2 and x_CA^2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response derives that the three x-components of the side vectors of an equilateral projected triangle satisfy sum of squares 3/2, then uses x_AB^2=3/4 to get x_BC^2+x_CA^2=3/4, and concludes the remaining two squares are both 3/8. Minor sign differences are irrelevant.
hints: Treat the side vectors in one projection as three unit vectors separated by 120° whose x-components are exactly the spatial side x-differences.
Because one squared x-component is 3/4 and the total is 3/2, the other two squared x-components are forced to be equal and sum to 3/4.

milestones_recovered.description: For each side UV of triangle ABC, suppose both orthogonal projections onto two perpendicular planes are segments of length 1, and let x_{UV} be the difference of the coordinates along the line of intersection of the two planes. Then |UV|^2=2-x_{UV}^2. Using x_AB^2=3/4 and x_BC^2=x_CA^2=3/8, compute the three side lengths AB, BC, CA.
milestones_recovered.answer: AB=sqrt(5)/2, BC=CA=sqrt(13/8)=sqrt(26)/4.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response computes BC^2=CA^2=2-3/8=13/8 and hence BC=CA=sqrt(13/8)=sqrt(26)/4, while retaining AB=sqrt(5)/2.
hints: Use |UV|^2=2-x_{UV}^2 for each side separately.
After obtaining BC^2=CA^2=13/8, take positive square roots because side lengths are positive.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the computed side lengths AB=sqrt(5)/2 and BC=CA=sqrt(26)/4 are geometrically plausible. Verify two things: first, each side length is between 1 and sqrt(2), which must hold because a segment whose projections onto two perpendicular planes both have length 1 satisfies |UV|^2=2-x_{UV}^2 with 0<=x_{UV}^2<=1; second, the triangle inequality holds for the side triple.
milestones_recovered.answer: All checks pass: 1<sqrt(5)/2<sqrt(2), 1<sqrt(26)/4<sqrt(2), and sqrt(5)/2<2·sqrt(26)/4 while the other triangle inequalities are automatic since all sides are positive.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response verifies the numerical bounds and at least one nontrivial triangle inequality explicitly. Equivalent exact comparisons are fine.
hints: Compare squares to avoid decimal approximations: 1<5/4<2 and 1<26/16<2.
For the triangle inequality, it is enough to show the largest side is less than the sum of the other two.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the established facts: AB=sqrt(5)/2, and the other two sides are BC=CA=sqrt(26)/4. Assemble these values to give the perimeter of triangle ABC, with no new geometric arguments.
milestones_recovered.answer: Perimeter = sqrt(5)/2 + sqrt(26)/2 = (sqrt(5)+sqrt(26))/2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final perimeter is given as sqrt(5)/2+2·sqrt(26)/4 and simplified to (sqrt(5)+sqrt(26))/2.
hints: Add AB+BC+CA.
Combine the two equal terms sqrt(26)/4+sqrt(26)/4 into sqrt(26)/2.

milestones_recovered.description: Model the toothpick structure as the grid graph formed by a 3-by-5 rectangle of unit segments: there are 4 horizontal grid lines each containing 5 toothpicks and 6 vertical grid lines each containing 3 toothpicks, for a total of 38 toothpicks. Show that the time when the whole structure has burned is 10 times the largest, over all toothpicks, of the minimum graph-distance from either A or B to a point of that toothpick, plus 1 extra toothpick-length for the toothpick itself to finish burning after ignition reaches its first point.

For any grid vertex (x,y), prove that the earliest time when fire reaches that vertex is 10·(x+min(y,3-y)) seconds, because any path from A or B to (x,y) needs x horizontal moves and then the smaller of y or 3-y vertical moves. milestones_recovered.answer: Vertex arrival distance is d(x,y)=x+min(y,3-y). A horizontal edge at height y starting at x is first reached at distance x+min(y,3-y). A vertical edge between y and y+1 at column x is first reached at distance x+min(y,2-y).

- a horizontal toothpick from (x,y) to (x+1,y), where x=0,1,2,3,4 and y=0,1,2,3, is first reached at distance x+min(y,3-y);
- a vertical toothpick from (x,y) to (x,y+1), where x=0,1,2,3,4,5 and y=0,1,2, is first reached at distance min(x+min(y,3-y), x+min(y+1,3-(y+1))).

milestones_recovered.description: Let the distinct real roots of the quadratic equation x^2+bx+c=0 be r and s. Use Vieta’s formulas r+s=-b and rs=c. The extra condition says one root equals the square-plus-itself of the other: either r=s^2+s or s=r^2+r. Show that, after renaming the roots if needed, the problem is equivalent to taking x_2=t, x_1=t^2+t for some real number t with t^2+t\ne t, and then expressing b and c by b=-(t^2+2t) and c=t(t^2+t)=t^3+t^2.
milestones_recovered.answer: A valid parametrization is x_2=t, x_1=t^2+t with t\in\mathbb R and t\notin\{-1,0\}; then b=-(t^2+2t), c=t^3+t^2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response introduces one real parameter t for one root, sets the other root equal to t^2+t, excludes equality of roots by t^2+t\ne t, and derives b and c via Vieta as b=-(t^2+2t), c=t^3+t^2. ALLOW swapping the names of the roots.

Then use the sum and product of roots to derive formulas for b and c in terms of t.

milestones_recovered.description:
Assume the quadratic x^2+bx+c=0 has distinct real roots x_2=t and x_1=t^2+t for some real t with t\notin\{-1,0\}. Using b=-(t^2+2t) and c=t^3+t^2, solve the condition b+c=4. That is, solve
-(t^2+2t)+(t^3+t^2)=4,
find all real t that also satisfy t\notin\{-1,0\}, and then compute the corresponding pairs (b,c).
milestones_recovered.answer: The equation becomes t^3-2t=4, i.e. t^3-2t-4=0=(t-2)(t^2+2t+2). The only real solution is t=2. Hence b=-(4+4)=-8 and c=8+4=12.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response reduces to t^3-2t-4=0, factors it correctly, notes the quadratic factor has no real roots, and obtains the unique pair (b,c)=(-8,12).
hints: Substitute the parametrized formulas for b and c into b+c=4 and simplify.
Check whether the cubic has an obvious integer root before factoring completely.

milestones_recovered.description: Verify directly that the pair b=-8 and c=12 found from the condition b+c=4 really satisfies the original problem. Check that x^2-8x+12=0 has two distinct real roots, and that one root equals the square-plus-itself of the other.
milestones_recovered.answer: x^2-8x+12=(x-2)(x-6), so the roots are 2 and 6, distinct and real. Also 6=2^2+2, so the condition holds.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver factors the quadratic or computes the roots and checks 6=2^2+2. ALLOW the equivalent check 2 is not equal to 6^2+6, since only one direction is needed.
hints: Factor x^2-8x+12.
Compare the two roots using the relation larger root = smaller root squared plus itself.

milestones_recovered.description: Assume the quadratic x^2+bx+c=0 has distinct real roots x_2=t and x_1=t^2+t for some real t with t\notin\{-1,0\}. Suppose now that b and c are coprime integers, meaning gcd(b,c)=1. Since b=-(t^2+2t) and c=t^3+t^2=t^2(t+1), use the facts that b and c are integers to show that t must be an integer. You may use this standard fact: if a rational number in lowest terms is u/v with gcd(u,v)=1 and v>0, then u^2+2uv divisible by v^2 implies v=1.
milestones_recovered.answer: t must be an integer.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response proves t is rational from t^2+2t=-b and then upgrades rational to integer, e.g. by writing t=(-2\pm\sqrt{4-4b})/2=-1\pm\sqrt{1-b} so t is rational because 1-b is an integer square, or by a lowest-terms denominator argument from t^2+2t\in\mathbb Z and t^2(t+1)\in\mathbb Z. The conclusion must be t\in\mathbb Z.
decompose_advice: First derive from b integer that t satisfies t^2+2t=-b, so (t+1)^2=1-b is an integer.
Conclude that t+1 is rational and in fact an integer square root shift, hence t is integer; alternatively use a lowest-terms denominator argument.

milestones_recovered.description: Let t be an integer with t\notin\{-1,0\}. Define b=-(t^2+2t) and c=t^3+t^2=t^2(t+1). Determine all integers t for which b and c are coprime integers, i.e. gcd(b,c)=1. Your output should be all resulting pairs (b,c).
milestones_recovered.answer: Since b=-t(t+2) and c=t^2(t+1), we have gcd(b,c) divisible by |t|. Thus |t|=1. The value t=-1 is forbidden because the roots would coincide, so t=1. Then b=-3 and c=2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response shows gcd(b,c)\ge |t|, concludes |t|=1 from coprimeness, excludes t=-1 because x_1=x_2= -1? More precisely t=-1 gives x_1=t^2+t=0 and x_2=-1, but c=0 and gcd not 1, or because t is excluded from distinctness parametrization; either route is fine if consistent. Then t=1 gives (b,c)=(-3,2).
hints: Factor b and c completely in terms of t: b=-t(t+2), c=t^2(t+1).
Notice that any common divisor of t and t^2 is a common divisor of b and c; coprimeness forces very small values of t.

milestones_recovered.description: Verify directly that the integer pair b=-3, c=2 satisfies all conditions in part (b): x^2+bx+c=0 has two distinct real roots, one root equals the square-plus-itself of the other, and gcd(b,c)=1.
milestones_recovered.answer: x^2-3x+2=(x-1)(x-2), so the roots are 1 and 2, distinct and real. Also 2=1^2+1, and gcd(-3,2)=1.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF all three checks appear: factorization or root computation, the relation 2=1^2+1, and coprimeness.
hints: Factor x^2-3x+2.
Check the relation between the two roots and compute gcd(3,2).

milestones_recovered.description: Using the established parametrization x_2=t, x_1=t^2+t with b=-(t^2+2t) and c=t^3+t^2: for part (a), the condition b+c=4 leads to the unique real parameter t=2 and therefore to (b,c)=(-8,12). For part (b), if b and c are coprime integers then t must be an integer, and coprimeness of b=-t(t+2) and c=t^2(t+1) forces t=1, giving (b,c)=(-3,2). State the final answers exactly.

milestones_recovered.description: Prove the following standalone fact: for any integer n>1, if p is the smallest prime divisor of n, then the largest proper divisor of n is n/p. Your proof should justify both that n/p is a divisor smaller than n and that any proper divisor d<n satisfies d\le n/p.
milestones_recovered.answer: If p is the smallest prime divisor of n, then the largest proper divisor of n equals n/p.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the proof shows n/p is a proper divisor and for any proper divisor d, the complementary factor n/d is an integer >1, hence has a prime divisor at least p, giving n/d\ge p and so d\le n/p.
hints: For any proper divisor d of n, the quotient n/d is an integer greater than 1.
Because p is the smallest prime divisor of n, every integer >1 dividing n is at least p.

milestones_recovered.description: Use this fact: if p is the smallest prime divisor of n>1, then the largest proper divisor of n is n/p. Combine it with the problem condition that the largest proper divisor is n-6. Derive an equation relating n and p, and solve it to express n in terms of p.
milestones_recovered.answer: n/p=n-6, so n(p-1)=6p and therefore n=6p/(p-1)=6+6/(p-1).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the equation n/p=n-6 is set up and rearranged correctly to n=6p/(p-1)=6+6/(p-1).
hints: Start from largest proper divisor = n/p and set it equal to n-6.
After rearranging, isolate n and write the result in a form that makes integrality easy to test.

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose n is a positive integer satisfying n=6+6/(p-1), where p is the smallest prime divisor of n. Since p is prime and n must be an integer, determine all possible primes p for which 6/(p-1) is an integer, compute the corresponding n, and keep only those cases where p is indeed the smallest prime divisor of that n.
milestones_recovered.answer: Possible primes from p-1\mid 6 are p=2,3,7, giving n=12,9,7 respectively; all three are valid.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response lists p=2,3,7 and corresponding n=12,9,7, and checks smallest-prime-divisor consistency for each.
decompose_advice: First solve p-1\mid 6 under the condition that p is prime.
Then verify for each candidate n that p actually is the smallest prime factor and that n/p=n-6.

milestones_recovered.description: Check directly that each candidate n in the set {7,9,12} satisfies the original condition 'n-6 is the second largest factor of n' by listing its positive divisors and identifying the second largest one.
milestones_recovered.answer: 7 has divisors 1,7 so no proper second-largest issue? Actually second largest factor is 1=7-6; 9 has divisors 1,3,9 so second largest is 3=9-6; 12 has divisors 1,2,3,4,6,12 so second largest is 6=12-6.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the divisors are correctly listed and the second largest factor is checked as 1,3,6 for n=7,9,12 respectively.
hints: For a prime number 7, the only positive divisors are 1 and 7, so the second largest is 1.
Verify explicitly that 1=7-6, 3=9-6, and 6=12-6.

milestones_recovered.description: Using the verified solutions n=7, 9, and 12, compute C as the sum of all positive integers n such that n-6 is the second largest factor of n. Then compute 11C.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final computation gives C=28 and 11C=308 exactly.
hints: Add the valid n-values to get C.
Multiply the result by 11.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider a pyramid with base area B>0, total surface area S, and lateral surface area L=S-B. Let φ be the common angle between each lateral face and the base plane. Because each lateral face makes the same angle with the base plane, the foot of the altitude from the apex to the base plane is equidistant from all sides of the base; denote this common distance in the base plane by r, and denote the pyramid height by h. Let P be the perimeter of the base. Show that the base must have an incircle of radius r, that B=(1/2)Pr, and that the sum of the areas of the lateral faces is L=(1/2)Pℓ, where ℓ is the common distance from the apex to any base edge measured inside the corresponding lateral face.
milestones_recovered.answer: With r the inradius of the base and ℓ the common slant height to the base edges, B=(1/2)Pr and L=(1/2)Pℓ.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response models the equal-dihedral-angle condition by introducing the projection of the apex onto the base, identifies equal distances from that point to all base edges, and derives B=(1/2)Pr and L=(1/2)Pℓ.
decompose_advice: Explain why equal angles with the base imply h/d is the same for every base edge, where d is the distance from the foot of the altitude to that edge, hence all such distances are equal to r.
Decompose the area formulas by triangulating the base from the incenter and summing areas of lateral triangles with common altitude ℓ to each base edge.

milestones_recovered.description: Let a lateral face meet the base plane along a base edge e. Let O be the foot of the pyramid altitude on the base plane, and let H be the foot of the perpendicular from O to the line containing e. In the lateral face, let A be the apex and let M be the foot of the perpendicular from A to the line containing e. Then triangle AOM lies in a plane perpendicular to e, and triangle OHM is its orthogonal projection onto the base plane. Prove that the angle φ between the lateral face and the base plane satisfies tan φ = h/r and also cos φ = r/ℓ, where h=AO, r=OH, and ℓ=AM.
milestones_recovered.answer: tan φ=h/r and equivalently cos φ=r/ℓ.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response correctly relates the dihedral angle to the right triangle obtained in a plane perpendicular to a base edge, yielding tanφ=h/r, and/or uses ℓ^2=h^2+r^2 to obtain cosφ=r/ℓ.
decompose_advice: First isolate the 2D cross-section by a plane through AO perpendicular to the chosen base edge.
Then identify φ as the angle between the line AM in the face and its projection HM in the base plane, and use the right triangle with legs h and r.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the formulas B=(1/2)Pr and L=(1/2)Pℓ from the model, together with cos φ = r/ℓ. Derive a direct relation between the given ratio k=S/B and the angle φ, where S=B+L is the total surface area.
milestones_recovered.answer: k=1+ℓ/r=1+sec φ.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response computes L/B=((1/2)Pℓ)/((1/2)Pr)=ℓ/r and then substitutes ℓ/r=secφ to get k=1+secφ.
hints: Since S=B+L, we have k=S/B=1+L/B.
From B=(1/2)Pr and L=(1/2)Pℓ, the quotient L/B is simply ℓ/r.

milestones_recovered.description: Solve the equation k=1+sec φ for the common angle φ between a lateral face and the base plane, and determine exactly for which real values of k such an angle exists for a pyramid. Here φ must be an angle between two intersecting planes of a nondegenerate pyramid, so 0<φ<π/2.
milestones_recovered.answer: φ=arccos(1/(k-1)), and a solution exists exactly for k≥2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response gives φ=arccos(1/(k-1)) and identifies the condition k-1≥1, i.e. k≥2, using 0<φ<π/2 so secφ≥1. Equivalent statements such as k∈[2,∞) are allowed.
decompose_advice: Use secφ=k-1, then invert with cosine.
For existence, use that 0<φ<π/2 implies cosφ∈(0,1] and secφ∈[1,∞).

milestones_recovered.description: Check the boundary behavior of the formula k=1+sec φ for a pyramid. Evaluate what happens as φ approaches 0 and as φ approaches π/2 from below, and use this to confirm the admissible range of k.
milestones_recovered.answer: As φ→0, secφ→1 so k→2; as φ→(π/2)^-, secφ→∞ so k→∞. Hence k ranges over [2,∞).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response states the two limits and concludes that the range of k is [2,∞).
hints: Use secφ=1/cosφ.
For acute φ, cosφ decreases from 1 toward 0, so secφ increases from 1 to ∞.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the established facts: the equal-angle condition implies the base has an incircle of radius r and B=(1/2)Pr, while the lateral area is L=(1/2)Pℓ; the common angle φ satisfies cos φ=r/ℓ. Therefore k=S/B=1+L/B=1+ℓ/r=1+sec φ. Conclude the requested angle and the exact condition on k for solvability.
milestones_recovered.answer: The common angle is φ=arccos(1/(k-1)). The problem has a solution exactly for k≥2.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the final answer exactly states the angle formula and the existence condition k≥2. Equivalent notation such as [2,∞) is allowed.
hints: From k=1+secφ, isolate secφ and then take arccos after reciprocating.
Use that for a pyramid the angle between a lateral face and the base is in (0,π/2), so secφ≥1.

milestones_recovered.description: List all possible squares whose vertices lie in S = {(i,j) : 0 ≤ i,j ≤ 3}. milestones_recovered.answer: There are exactly 20 squares in S: 14 axis-parallel squares and 6 tilted squares. parent_prompt:
Solve the following problem. Provide your final answer clearly.

Problem:
## 31. Child Psychology

The brightest hours of a little child are spent drawing. He depicts either Indians or Eskimos and draws a dwelling for each of them. Thus, next to the Eskimos, he usually draws an igloo, and next to the Indians - wigwams. But sometimes the child makes mistakes, and not infrequently, for example, an igloo can be found next to the Indians. One female psychologist took these drawings very seriously; she discovered that there were twice as many Indians as Eskimos, that there were as many Eskimos with wigwams as there were Indians with igloos, and that, speaking of Eskimos, one wigwam corresponds to three igloos. Then she decided to determine what part of the inhabitants of the wigwams are Indians (and derive from this a very complex IQ $^{1}$ formula for the child). But she has not yet succeeded in doing so. Help her.
parent_note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description: Let E_i be the number of Eskimos drawn with igloos, E_w the number of Eskimos drawn with wigwams, I_i the number of Indians drawn with igloos, and I_w the number of Indians drawn with wigwams. Translate the story into equations using these variables. The story says: the total number of Indians is twice the total number of Eskimos; the number of Eskimos with wigwams equals the number of Indians with igloos; and among Eskimos, the number with wigwams is one third of the number with igloos because one wigwam corresponds to three igloos. Also state the target quantity: the fraction of wigwam inhabitants who are Indians.
milestones_recovered.answer: Equations: I_i + I_w = 2(E_i + E_w), E_w = I_i, and E_w : E_i = 1 : 3 (equivalently E_i = 3E_w). Target: I_w / (I_w + E_w).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the four variables are defined correctly, the three conditions are encoded correctly, and the target fraction is identified as Indians among all wigwam inhabitants.
decompose_advice: Introduce one variable for each people/dwelling combination.
Be explicit that total Indians means I_i + I_w and total Eskimos means E_i + E_w.

milestones_recovered.description: Using only the system of equations I_i + I_w = 2(E_i + E_w), E_w = I_i, and E_i = 3E_w, solve for I_w in terms of E_w and then compute the fraction of wigwam inhabitants who are Indians, namely I_w / (I_w + E_w).
milestones_recovered.answer: I_w = 7E_w, so I_w / (I_w + E_w) = 7E_w / 8E_w = 7/8.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver substitutes E_i = 3E_w and I_i = E_w into the first equation to get E_w + I_w = 2(3E_w + E_w) = 8E_w, hence I_w = 7E_w, and then obtains 7/8.
hints: Substitute I_i = E_w into the equation for total Indians.
Since E_i = 3E_w, the total number of Eskimos is 4E_w.

milestones_recovered.description: Check that the computed ratio is consistent by building one concrete integer example. Use a positive integer t and set E_w = t, E_i = 3t, I_i = t, and I_w = 7t. Verify all three story conditions and the final fraction of wigwam inhabitants who are Indians.
milestones_recovered.answer: The conditions hold: Indians = I_i + I_w = 8t and Eskimos = E_i + E_w = 4t, so Indians are twice Eskimos; Eskimos with wigwams equal Indians with igloos since both are t; among Eskimos, wigwams:igloos = t:3t = 1:3. Wigwam inhabitants total 8t, of whom 7t are Indians, so the fraction is 7/8.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF a consistent example equivalent to t=1 or any positive multiple is given and it confirms the ratio 7/8.
hints: Take the smallest choice t = 1 if desired.
Then the four counts are 1, 3, 1, 7 in the order E_w, E_i, I_i, I_w.

Problem:
7.51 There are 1988 identical unit cubes, and they (all or part) are used to form three "squares" $A, B, C$ (i.e., three one-layer-high rectangular prisms with dimensions $a \times a \times 1, b \times b \times 1, c \times c \times 1$ where $a \leqslant b \leqslant c$). Now, place square $C$ on a flat surface, then place $B$ on top of $C$ so that each small block of $B$ is exactly on top of a small block of $C$, but the boundary of $B$ does not align with the sides of $C$. Finally, place $A$ on top of $B$ following the same principle, thus forming a "three-story building". When $a, b, c$ take what values, can the number of different "three-story buildings" that can be formed be maximized?

milestones_recovered.description: Let the three one-layer squares have side lengths a,b,c in unit cubes, with positive integers a <= b <= c. Using some or all of 1988 unit cubes means a^2 + b^2 + c^2 <= 1988. A placement of B on top of C is allowed when every unit cube of B sits directly above a unit cube of C, but the boundary of B is not aligned with the sides of C; equivalently, if the lower-left corner of B is shifted by integers (x,y) relative to the lower-left corner of C, then 0 <= x <= c-b, 0 <= y <= c-b, and x,y are not both in {0,c-b}. The number of such placements is (c-b+1)^2-4 when c>b, and 0 when c=b. Similarly, for A on top of B the number is (b-a+1)^2-4 when b>a, and 0 when b=a. Therefore the number of different three-story buildings determined by (a,b,c) is N(a,b,c)=((b-a+1)^2-4)((c-b+1)^2-4), under a^2+b^2+c^2<=1988 and strict inequalities a<b<c if we want N>0. Restate the optimization problem in these terms.

parent_prompt:
Solve the following problem. Provide your final answer clearly.

Problem:
Example 1 A class has 6 lessons scheduled on Monday: Chinese, Math, History, PE, Music, and Geography.
(1) If PE cannot be scheduled as the first lesson in the morning, History cannot be the second lesson in the morning, Music cannot be the third lesson in the morning, Chinese cannot be the fourth lesson in the morning, Math cannot be the first lesson in the afternoon, and Geography cannot be the second lesson in the afternoon, how many different schedules are there?
(2) In question (1), if the condition “Music cannot be the third lesson in the morning” is changed to “Music must be the third lesson in the morning,” how many different schedules are there?
(3) In question (1), if the condition “Geography cannot be the second lesson in the afternoon” is removed, how many different schedules are there?
(4) In question (1), if the restrictions “Math cannot be the first lesson in the afternoon, Geography cannot be the second lesson in the afternoon” are removed, how many different schedules are there?
(5) In question (1), if the condition “Music cannot be the third lesson in the morning” is changed to “Music must be the third or fourth lesson in the morning,” how many different schedules are there?
(6) Chinese, Math, History, and Geography must be scheduled in the morning, the first lesson cannot be History, the second lesson cannot be Geography, the third lesson cannot be Chinese, and the fourth lesson cannot be Math. How many different schedules are there?
(7) Chinese, Math, and History must be scheduled in the first to third lessons in the morning, but the first lesson in the morning cannot be History, the second lesson in the morning cannot be Chinese, the third lesson in the morning cannot be Math, the fourth lesson in the morning cannot be PE, the first lesson in the afternoon cannot be Music, and the second lesson in the afternoon cannot be Geography. How many different schedules are there?
parent_note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description: Place rectangle ABCD on the coordinate plane with A=(0,0), B=(6,0), C=(6,8), and D=(0,8), so AB=6 and BC=8. Let E be a point on side AD, so write E=(0,t) for some real number t with 0<=t<=8. The rectangle is folded along line CE, and under this fold point D reflects to point F. Because F lies on diagonal AC, and diagonal AC is the line through A=(0,0) and C=(6,8), any point on AC has equation y=(4/3)x. Also, reflection across line CE preserves distance from C, so CF=CD=6. Express the condition that F is on AC and at distance 6 from C, and find the possible coordinates of F.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer identifies the nontrivial point on y=(4/3)x satisfying distance 6 from C=(6,8); allow also listing the other intersection F=C and excluding it because D does not fold to C.

milestones_recovered.description: Use the coordinate setup A=(0,0), B=(6,0), C=(6,8), D=(0,8), E=(0,t), and F=(12/5,16/5). Since folding along line CE reflects D to F, the fold line CE is the perpendicular bisector of segment DF. Therefore point E lies on the perpendicular bisector of DF, so ED=EF. Compute t from the equation ED=EF, where ED=8-t and EF is the distance from E=(0,t) to F=(12/5,16/5).

Let the small ball have center P=(x,y,z) and radius r. Because the ball lies inside the cube [0,1]^3, the distances from P to the six faces are x, 1-x, y, 1-y, z, 1-z, so the condition that the whole ball stays inside the cube is r<=x, r<=1-x, r<=y, r<=1-y, r<=z, r<=1-z. The segment AC1 is the space diagonal from A=(0,0,0) to C1=(1,1,1), that is, the set of points (t,t,t) for 0<=t<=1. milestones_recovered.answer: Maximize r subject to 0<=x,y,z<=1, r<=min{x,1-x,y,1-y,z,1-z}, and dist((x,y,z), AC1)=r, where AC1={(t,t,t):0<=t<=1}.

Also show that if 0<=x,y,z<=1 then the orthogonal projection of P onto L is the point (m,m,m) with 0<=m<=1, so the distance from P to the segment AC1={(t,t,t):0<=t<=1} is the same number.
milestones_recovered.answer: For L in direction (1,1,1), the closest point to P is (m,m,m) with m=(x+y+z)/3, hence dist(P,AC1)^2=dist(P,L)^2=(x-m)^2+(y-m)^2+(z-m)^2 because 0<=m<=1 whenever 0<=x,y,z<=1.

Separately verify 0<=m<=1 from 0<=x,y,z<=1.

Show that the translated coordinates u=x-1/2, v=y-1/2, w=z-1/2 satisfy |u|,|v|,|w|<=1/2-s, hence u^2+v^2+w^2<=3(1/2-s)^2. Using the lemma that the distance from P to the line x=y=z equals the distance from (u,v,w) to the parallel line through the origin, prove that dist(P,AC1)^2<=2(1/2-s)^2. Conclude that any feasible radius satisfies r<=sqrt(2)(1/2-r), and therefore r<=sqrt(2)-1.

decompose_advice: For fixed s, maximize the distance to the diagonal under the box constraints s<=x,y,z<=1-s by translating to the cube centered at (1/2,1/2,1/2).
Use the identity distance squared to the diagonal equals |(u,v,w)|^2-((u+v+w)^2/3), then bound this by |(u,v,w)|^2.

milestones_recovered.description: Show that the upper bound r<=sqrt(2)-1 is attainable. Consider the point P=(r,r,1-r) with r=sqrt(2)-1. Next compute the distance from P to the segment AC1={(t,t,t):0<=t<=1}. Using m=(x+y+z)/3, show that for P=(r,r,1-r), the closest point on the diagonal is (1/3+r/3,1/3+r/3,1/3+r/3), and the squared distance is 2(1/2-r)^2. milestones_recovered.answer: For P=(r,r,1-r) with r=sqrt(2)-1, the ball is inside the cube and dist(P,AC1)=r, so the bound is attained.

Since AC=BD=√(x^2+y^2), AB=CD=x, BC=y, and the given products are PA·PB=2, PB·PC=9, PC·PD=18, derive a system relating x,y to the three unknown lengths PA,PB,PC,PD.

milestones_recovered.description: Solve both parts by translating each word problem into equations. Part G10.1: for a regular polygon with n sides, the sum of one exterior angle and one interior angle is 180 degrees, and the sum of all exterior angles is 360 degrees. Write an equation using the given interior angle 160 degrees to determine n. Part G10.2: if the n-th day of May is Friday and the k-th day of May is Tuesday in the same year, then the number of days from day n to day k is k-n, and weekdays advance by that many days modulo 7. Tuesday is 4 days after Friday modulo 7 because Friday->Saturday->Sunday->Monday->Tuesday is 4 steps. Also 20<k<26, so k is one of 21,22,23,24,25. Express the condition on k-n modulo 7 and identify what k must satisfy.
milestones_recovered.answer: G10.1 gives an equation for n from exterior angle 20 degrees; G10.2 gives k-n ≡ 4 (mod 7) with k in {21,22,23,24,25}.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the setup includes exterior angle = 180-160=20 and/or interior-angle formula equivalent to n; and for dates includes weekday difference 4 mod 7 and the range 21 to 25 for k.
decompose_advice: For G10.1, isolate the polygon calculation into a separate computation milestone.
For G10.2, split into one milestone that converts weekday difference into a modular condition and another that tests candidates 21 through 25.

milestones_recovered.description: Part G10.1 only. In a regular polygon, one exterior angle equals 180 degrees minus one interior angle. The sum of all exterior angles is 360 degrees. Here each interior angle is 160 degrees, so each exterior angle is 20 degrees. Compute the number n of sides from n times 20 degrees = 360 degrees.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the result is 18, whether obtained from 360/20 or from the equivalent interior-angle formula ((n-2)180)/n=160.

milestones_recovered.answer: k - n ≡ 4 (mod 7)
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver states Tuesday is 4 days after Friday modulo 7, hence k-n is congruent to 4 mod 7.
hints: Count forward from Friday to Tuesday.
Use modulo 7 because weekdays repeat every 7 days.

Since weekday labels for May dates are determined mod 7, Tuesday dates in that interval are exactly those congruent to the Friday date plus 4 modulo 7. hints: Among 21,22,23,24,25, only one has the correct residue class for Tuesday relative to Friday.

parent_prompt:
Solve the following problem. Provide your final answer clearly.

Problem:
There is a chessboard with $m$ columns and $n$ rows. In each blanks, an integer is given. If a rectangle $R$ (in this chessboard) has an integer $h$ satisfying the following two conditions, we call $R$ as a 'shelf'. 
(i) All integers contained in $R$ are bigger than $h$.
(ii) All integers in blanks, which are not contained in $R$ but meet with $R$ at a vertex or a side, are not bigger than $h$.
Assume that all integers are given to make shelves as much as possible. Find the number of shelves.
parent_note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

Original problem (context):
There is a chessboard with $m$ columns and $n$ rows. In each blanks, an integer is given. If a rectangle $R$ (in this chessboard) has an integer $h$ satisfying the following two conditions, we call $R$ as a 'shelf'. 
(i) All integers contained in $R$ are bigger than $h$.
(ii) All integers in blanks, which are not contained in $R$ but meet with $R$ at a vertex or a side, are not bigger than $h$.
Assume that all integers are given to make shelves as much as possible. Find the number of shelves.

Milestone:
An outer frame has dimensions (m+1)\times(n+1). Compute its area as a simplified expression.

Output instruction: Put the area expression in \boxed{}.
milestones_original.note: ACCEPT IF equivalent final answer is present.

milestones_recovered.description: Let the chessboard be the set of cells with coordinates (i,j), where 1\le i\le m is the column index and 1\le j\le n is the row index. A rectangle means any axis-parallel subboard consisting of all cells (i,j) with a\le i\le b and c\le j\le d for some integers a,b,c,d. For such a rectangle R, define its neighboring cells to be all cells not in R that share either a side or a vertex with at least one cell of R. Restate the shelf condition precisely in terms of the values on cells of R and on the neighboring cells of R, and state the optimization goal of the original problem.
milestones_recovered.answer: A rectangle R is a shelf iff there exists an integer h such that every cell in R has value > h and every neighboring cell of R has value \le h. Equivalently, max(value on neighboring cells) < min(value on cells of R). The goal is to assign integers to the m\times n cells so that the number of rectangles R satisfying this condition is as large as possible, and then determine that maximum number.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the answer formalizes shelf as a strict separation between inside cells and all side/vertex neighboring outside cells, and states the maximization target over all rectangles.
decompose_advice: Make explicit why existence of an integer h is equivalent to a strict inequality between the largest neighboring value and the smallest interior value.
Separate the geometric definition of rectangle/neighbor from the optimization objective.

milestones_recovered.description: Prove the following standalone claim. For any rectangle R in the m\times n board, write B(R) for the set consisting of all cells of R together with all cells not in R that share a side or vertex with a cell of R. Then R is a shelf exactly when every cell of R has value strictly larger than every cell in B(R)\setminus R. In particular, if all cell values are distinct, R is a shelf exactly when the cells of R are precisely the |R| largest values among the cells of B(R).
milestones_recovered.answer: The first statement is equivalent to the definition because there exists an integer h with outside neighbors \le h < inside cells exactly when max(B(R)\setminus R) < min(R). If all values are distinct, that strict separation means every value on R exceeds every value in B(R)\setminus R, so the cells of R are exactly the top |R| values inside B(R), and conversely.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF both equivalences are justified. Distinctness only needs to be used for the reformulation as 'top |R| values in B(R)'.
hints: Use h between the largest neighboring value and the smallest value in R.
For distinct values, 'every inside value is larger than every outside-neighbor value' is the same as saying the inside cells are the largest |R| values in the union.

milestones_recovered.description: Consider assigning pairwise distinct integers to the cells by ranking them from largest to smallest. Show that such an assignment determines, for each k=1,2,\dots,mn, a set S_k consisting of the k cells with largest values. Prove the following two facts: (1) if some rectangle R with |R|=k is a shelf, then S_k=R; (2) therefore, in any assignment with distinct values, for each k there can be at most one shelf of area k. Conclude that the total number of shelves is at most mn.
milestones_recovered.answer: For a shelf R of area k, the previous lemma shows that within B(R) the k cells of R are exactly the k largest values there. Since every cell outside R but adjacent to R has smaller value than every cell of R, when the global values are listed from largest downward, the first k cells must be exactly R; hence S_k=R. Therefore two different shelves cannot have the same area k, because both would have to equal S_k. So there is at most one shelf for each k, and the total number of shelves is at most mn.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the milestone establishes uniqueness by area and the global upper bound mn. Minor variation in the justification of S_k=R is fine as long as it is explicit.
decompose_advice: If needed, isolate the statement 'a shelf of area k is uniquely determined by the top k cells' as a subclaim.
If the jump from uniqueness-by-area to the bound mn is too fast, split it into a separate counting step.

milestones_recovered.description:
Construct an explicit labeling of the m\times n board that produces a shelf of every possible area k=1,2,\dots,mn. One candidate is the row-major increasing labeling
v(i,j)=(j-1)m+i,
so the bottom row has values 1,2,\dots,m, the next row has m+1,\dots,2m, and the top-right corner has value mn. For each k, write k=qm+r with integers q\ge 0 and 0\le r<m. Define R_k to be the set of the top q full rows together with, if r>0, the leftmost r cells of the next row below those q rows. Prove that R_k is a rectangle and is a shelf of area k.
milestones_recovered.answer: Using the labeling v(i,j)=(j-1)m+i, the k largest cells form a Ferrers shape taken from the top rows and the next row. To make it a rectangle, take instead the equivalent decreasing labeling from top-left to bottom-right: w(i,j)=(n-j)m+(m-i+1). Then for k=qm+r with 0\le r<m, the top q full rows together with the leftmost r cells of the next row are exactly the k largest cells, which form the rectangle with rows j=n-q+1 to n and, if r>0, extended one more row on columns 1 to r; equivalently, for every rectangle anchored at the top-left corner with area k, all cells inside have larger labels than every cell touching it from outside. Thus there is a shelf of each area k=1,2,\dots,mn.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver provides any explicit labeling with distinct integers and proves that for every k there is a shelf of area k. A standard valid construction is monotone decreasing away from one corner so that each corner-anchored rectangle is a shelf. The exact formula may differ.
decompose_advice: First choose a clean explicit formula, such as value(i,j)=-(Ai+j) with A large, or an order that decreases along rows and columns from one corner.
Then prove separately that every top-left-corner rectangle has all neighboring outside cells smaller than all inside cells.

Finally show that for each k between 1 and mn there exists a top-left-corner rectangle of area k in the chosen ordering, or modify the construction so the top k cells themselves always form a rectangle.

milestones_recovered.description: Give a correct explicit construction that achieves the upper bound mn. Define the value in cell (i,j) to be 2^{i+j}. For a rectangle R=[a,b]\times[c,d], compute the smallest value inside R and the largest value among cells not in R but sharing a side or vertex with R, in the special case a=1 and c=1, that is, R is the top-left corner rectangle {1\le i\le b, 1\le j\le d}. Then prove such a corner rectangle is a shelf. Finally, explain why for every area k with 1\le k\le mn there exists exactly one top-left corner rectangle of area k if and only if k can be written as bd for some 1\le b\le m and 1\le d\le n; if this does not cover all k, refine the construction idea so that one gets a shelf for every k and state the resulting maximum number of shelves.
milestones_recovered.answer: For values 2^{i+j}, a top-left corner rectangle has all inside cells with i+j\le b+d, while any adjacent outside cell has i+j\ge b+d+1, so this choice makes the inside smaller, not larger, and does not solve the problem. Thus a product-type or sum-type monotone corner labeling only creates shelves for some areas, not all mn. A refined construction is needed: choose distinct values so that for each k, the set of the k largest cells is itself a rectangle. Then by the lemma that set is a shelf, giving one shelf for every k and achieving mn total shelves.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver notices the proposed naive construction fails and correctly states the needed refined criterion: for each k, the top k cells should form a rectangle. This milestone is a checkpoint toward the final construction, not the final proof.
decompose_advice: Test a simple monotone construction on a 2x2 or 3x3 board to see why it fails for some k.
Rephrase the construction goal as: order the cells so every initial segment is a rectangle.

milestones_recovered.description: Characterize total orders of the cells whose every initial segment is a rectangle. An initial segment means the set of the first k cells in the order, for some k. Prove that there exists an ordering of the mn cells such that for every k=1,2,\dots,mn, the first k cells form a rectangle: namely, snake through the board by columns of height n, but this does not work because initial segments are not always rectangles. Find and prove a correct ordering with this property, or prove that such an ordering exists by a recursive construction that adds one cell at a time while keeping the occupied set rectangular.
milestones_recovered.answer: A valid ordering exists: start from a 1\times1 rectangle and repeatedly enlarge the current rectangle by adding one whole new row or one whole new column, but list the newly added row or column in an order that keeps every partial addition rectangular. Recursively, if rectangles of all sizes exist in an a\times b board with nested initial segments, then appending a new last row or new last column extends the ordering to (a+1)\times b or a\times(b+1). Hence there is an ordering of the m\times n cells whose first k cells form a rectangle for every k.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the solver supplies a genuine recursive or explicit ordering with the nested-rectangle property for all k. Because this is the core constructive step, the verifier should require a clear argument that every k is covered.
decompose_advice: Handle the base case min(m,n)=1 first.
For the induction step, decide whether to remove a last row or a last column so that the remaining board is smaller, then append the removed strip after ordering the smaller board.
Within an appended strip, order its cells so each intermediate size still gives a rectangle.

milestones_recovered.description: Check the candidate final answer against small boards. Determine the maximum possible number of shelves for boards of sizes 1\times1, 1\times2, 2\times2, and 2\times3. For each case, compare with the formula suggested by the previous milestones.
milestones_recovered.answer: The counts are 1, 2, 4, and 6 respectively, matching the formula mn.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the listed small-case maxima equal mn and at least one brief justification is given, such as uniqueness by area plus an example assignment.
hints: For a 1\times n board, each interval can be a shelf only if it is an initial segment in a suitable ordering, so the maximum is n.
For 2\times2 and 2\times3, the upper bound by area already gives 4 and 6; check that constructions exist.

milestones_recovered.description: Assemble the conclusion using these established facts: (A) if values are distinct, a shelf of area k is uniquely determined by the set of the k largest values, so there is at most one shelf of each area k; hence any labeling has at most mn shelves in total; (B) there exists a labeling of the m\times n board for which, for every k=1,2,\dots,mn, the set of the k largest cells is a rectangle, and then that rectangle is a shelf. Using only (A) and (B), state the maximum possible number of shelves on an m\times n board.
milestones_recovered.answer: The maximum possible number of shelves is mn.
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the response combines the upper bound mn from uniqueness by area with a construction achieving mn, and concludes exactly mn.
hints: Upper bound: at most one shelf for each possible area 1 through mn.
Lower bound: a construction with one shelf of every area reaches mn.

milestones_recovered.description: Let x = AO and d = OO'. From the facts x^2 - 2015^2 = (x+d)^2 - 2016^2 and A,O,B,O' are collinear in that order, solve for x in terms of d. Simplify completely.

milestones_recovered.description: For two circles with centers O,O' and radii r,s, if a common tangent touches the first circle at T and the second at T', then the line OO' together with the tangent segment TT' forms a right triangle whose legs are |s-r| and TT' and whose hypotenuse is OO'. In the rectangular table shown below, the number $1$ is written in the upper-left hand corner, and every number is the sum of the any numbers directly to its left and above.  The table extends infinitely downwards and to the right.
\[ 
  \begin{array}{cccccc}
    1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots \\
    1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & \cdots \\
    1 & 3 & 6 & 10 & 15 & \cdots \\
    1 & 4 & 10 & 20 & 35 & \cdots \\

  \end{array}
\]
Wanda the Worm, who is on a diet after a feast two years ago, wants to eat $n$ numbers (not necessarily distinct in value) from the table such that the sum of the numbers is less than one million. However, she cannot eat two numbers in the same row or column (or both). What is the largest possible value of $n$?

milestones_recovered.description: Let the entry in row r and column c of the infinite table be T(r,c), where rows and columns are numbered starting at 0 in the upper-left corner. The table is defined by T(0,c)=1 for all c>=0, T(r,0)=1 for all r>=0, and for r,c>=1, T(r,c)=T(r-1,c)+T(r,c-1). Wanda may choose n entries T(r_1,c_1),...,T(r_n,c_n) such that no two chosen entries share a row or share a column. Restate the optimization problem in terms of the numbers T(r,c): identify a closed form for T(r,c), and reformulate the goal as maximizing the size of a set of pairs (r_i,c_i) with all r_i distinct and all c_i distinct, subject to the sum of the corresponding T(r_i,c_i) being less than 1,000,000.
milestones_recovered.answer: T(r,c)=binom(r+c,r)=binom(r+c,c), so the problem is to maximize n such that one can choose n pairs (r_i,c_i) with all r_i distinct and all c_i distinct and with sum Σ binom(r_i+c_i,r_i) < 1,000,000.

You may use Pascal's identity binom(m-1,k-1)+binom(m-1,k)=binom(m,k).
milestones_recovered.answer: For all r,c>=0, T(r,c)=binom(r+c,r).
milestones_recovered.note: ACCEPT IF the proof checks base cases r=0 or c=0 and uses the recurrence plus Pascal's identity for the inductive step. ALLOW binom(r+c,c) instead.
hints: Check the boundary values: binom(c,0)=1 and binom(r,r)=1.
For r,c>=1, verify binom(r+c-1,r-1)+binom(r+c-1,r)=binom(r+c,r).

milestones_recovered.description: Suppose you want to choose k positions (r_1,c_1),...,(r_k,c_k) in the nonnegative integer grid so that all chosen row indices are distinct and all chosen column indices are distinct. Assume the row indices are fixed and satisfy r_1<r_2<...<r_k. Show that among all possible choices of distinct nonnegative column indices, the sum Σ binom(r_i+c_i,r_i) is minimized when the column indices are exactly 0,1,2,...,k-1 in some order, and more specifically when after sorting the columns increasingly as d_1<d_2<...<d_k, one has d_j>=j-1 for every j.
milestones_recovered.answer: After sorting the chosen distinct columns as d_1<...<d_k, one must have d_j>=j-1; since binom(r+d,r) is increasing in d for fixed r, the minimum possible sum for fixed rows occurs when the columns are 0,1,...,k-1.

milestones_recovered.description: Let r_1<r_2<...<r_k be distinct nonnegative integers, and let d_1<d_2<...<d_k be distinct nonnegative integers. Prove that Σ_{i=1}^k binom(r_i+d_i,r_i) is at least Σ_{i=1}^k binom(2i-2,i-1). You may use two facts: (1) because r_1<...<r_k are distinct nonnegative integers, one has r_i>=i-1 for each i; (2) for fixed c, the value binom(r+c,r)=binom(r+c,c) is increasing in r, and for fixed r it is increasing in c.
milestones_recovered.answer: For all such strictly increasing sequences r_i and d_i, Σ binom(r_i+d_i,r_i) >= Σ binom(2i-2,i-1). Equality is achieved by r_i=i-1 and d_i=i-1 for all i.
